2485.title: Timeline for Base UI stable release
2485.body:
Hi Base UI team 👋
The latest version is currently `1.0.0-beta.2` and I noticed earlier blog posts had mentioned a possible stable release target in late 2024.

I was wondering:
- Is there an updated timeline or roadmap for when Base UI might reach its first stable (non-beta) release?
- Are there specific features or milestones you’re aiming to complete before calling 1.0 stable?

Knowing this would help me and my team plan migration from MUI Base to Base UI (or if we should consider  using other packages for our needs).

nodes.body:
> possible stable release target in late 2024

We hope to have a stable release this year, so "late 2025" 😬

@emjony Yep as Albert mentioned, v1 will be this year. Probably the October release. We don't have specific features/components in mind, it's more about stability, refining APIs, and consolidating APIs library-wide to minimise the risk of future breaking changes.

However, if you're on MUI Base, I'd highly recommend migrating asap, since Base UI is already much more feature-complete, has a lot fewer bugs, and has dramatically better a11y.
Thank you guys for your time and efforts on base ui. Love to use it.
This should be closed by https://github.com/mui/base-ui/commit/8319606c8b93366f83a1eb45c0970d106b7fa802 I think!

3530.title: Tooltip vs. Popover - touch support
3530.body:
Hi! I had a question about the rationale for having both Tooltip and Popover components.

From the docs, I understand that **Tooltip is meant for non-critical, supplementary information and that it won't appear on touch devices**. The **documentation even recommends using Popover for content that must be accessible everywhere**. Given that, I was curious about when Tooltip is expected to be the better choice in practice.

My **concern is more around ergonomics and potential misuse**: many developers are very familiar with the concept and naming of "tooltips" so they may naturally reach for Tooltip without fully considering its touch limitations, especially since **people usually don't choose tooltips specifically because they don't work on touch devices**. In practice, it seems **common sense to use a Popover with hover interactions as a "tooltip"**, since it also works on mobile.

I understand and appreciate the distinction here, especially that **Popover supports focus management, interactive content, and all input methods, while Tooltip is intentionally a purely visual, non-interactive affordance for sighted users and can safely coexist with other triggers**. That makes sense from an accessibility and composability standpoint. 
My question is more about day-to-day usage: in many cases, I (and likely others) just need a simple "tooltip-like" hint that also works on touch devices. 

I'm wondering if the separation is mainly conceptual (semantics, accessibility, intent), or if there are concrete scenarios where Tooltip is preferred over a hover-configured Popover, despite the mobile limitations.

Would love to understand the design reasoning here, **thanks for the great work on Base UI**!

nodes.body:
My own thoughts:

**Tooltips** are attached to and visually label a tool (like a button, input, or link) that performs its own action completely unrelated to the tooltip. The tooltip is an optional/secondary visual-only hint that users don't _need_ to see, but can help with clarity. Touch devices never see tooltips because they appear on hover of focus only. Nothing in the tooltip should be interactive unless the interactive content is non-critical or there are other means of accessing that content (see: description for Preview Card).

**Popovers with `openOnHover`** are attached to a button whose purpose is to show the popover itself (like an info icon that is _not optional_ - every user regardless of their input modality needs to see it). The button doesn't perform a separate action: its purpose is to show the popover, not do something else. Focus moves into the popover when it opens so users can navigate and read its text/content with a screen reader.

> in many cases, I (and likely others) just need a simple "tooltip-like" hint that also works on touch devices.

Do you have some examples of the UI you're trying to achieve?

Thanks for the explanation, this was really helpful. I appreciate you taking the time to clearly lay out the mental model and intent behind tooltips vs. popovers. In my current use case, for example, I'm using tooltips on icon buttons in a collapsed sidebar. In this case, the lack of touch support isn't a major issue, since those icon buttons aren't shown in a sidebar on touch devices and are instead rendered in a bottom navigation bar. While it's not a critical problem, **being able to touch and hold a navigation item (or other icon button) to see its tooltip or label would still add some quality-of-life value**, helping users understand the navigation item / icon button they're interacting with. I'm using popovers to show additional information about certain elements in the app (stats in a text-based RPG, see attached screenshots). There's no interactivity inside the popup and nothing strictly required, but the extra context can be helpful. Even so, I prefer that mobile users can still tap around and reveal those details, which is why popovers feel like a better fit in practice. That said, I do think this distinction is a point of confusion for many developers. There are quite a few discussions around this topic already, particularly in the context of the shadcn / Radix tooltip components, which suggests this isn't an isolated concern. https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui/issues/86#issuecomment-2241817826 https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui/issues/2402#issuecomment-1930895113

Note that iOS doesn't have tooltips anywhere in its OS (AFAICT). That probably means they aren't really necessary when using a touch device, as a mobile operating system used by 1+ billion people with a large ecosystem of apps also don't use tooltips in the UI. Needing a tooltip to be displayed on touch therefore might indicate there is a possible design mistake in your app that should be refactored into an alternative UI that more commonly is found in mobile apps.

Two cases I can think of where a tooltip might be valid, though:

1. Long press to open a tooltip could be valid, but other OS actions may be blocked to favor the tooltip. e.g. text selection, magnifying glass on iOS. When would this be a problem? It's also not really discoverable in the first place, at least for iOS users where long presses never show a tooltip anywhere in the OS
2. A button is disabled (`<Button focusableWhenDisabled />`) and it has a tooltip attached to it to inform the user why it's disabled. On touch devices, a click to show a tooltip becomes valid because the button doesn't do anything except show the tooltip given that it's disabled

---

In the examples you showed, there seem to be some solutions:

1. "Active Quests" can display that information inline on the route itself when clicked
2. "Charisma" at least on narrow screens can just be rendered as an inline visual label underneath or above the number field input

I think we may be talking slightly past each other, so apologies if I didn't communicate my point clearly earlier.

My main goal in opening this discussion was to better understand the **practical differences between Tooltip and Popover** - where each is intended to be used, and whether there are edge cases that aren't fully covered by the current model. I also floated a small, **optional idea around touch-and-hold tooltips** for simple icon buttons or navigation items, where the tooltip is purely descriptive, non-interactive, and not the primary action of the trigger.

I'm not trying to argue from an ideological standpoint about what should or shouldn't exist based on platform conventions (Apple vs. Android). Those comparisons can be interesting, but I don't think they're particularly conclusive in either direction. For example, I'm not sure about iOS, but Android does include tooltips at the OS level and has a user base of around 4 billion people, though I don't think that proves anything one way or the other.

I also want to be clear that I'm not asking for a must-have feature, nor am I criticizing the project or its direction, **I genuinely appreciate the work that's gone into it.**

Regarding my own design examples: I don't _need_ tooltips to work on touch. The information shown in them isn't on the critical path and is available elsewhere in the UI. **Supporting touch would simply be a small quality-of-life improvement in certain cases, not a requirement**.

Finally, I'm not convinced that wanting tooltip-like affordances to work on touch necessarily implies a flawed design. That framing feels a bit strong, and I'm mainly interested in having a constructive discussion about tradeoffs, intent, and potential gaps, rather than prescribing a single "correct" design approach.

The key issue is that on some platforms (like iOS), long pressing elements is often associated with a contextual action that opens a native popup. On iOS, long pressing a link opens a preview popup, so the tooltip would either get lost in view, or would underlap the native popup unexpectedly. This means long pressing to open a tooltip is not a safe action across all touch input.

On Android, it does support tooltips that open on long press, though I'm not sure what the differences are with iOS regarding its native contextual popups when long pressing elements in a browser.

My main point with iOS not supporting tooltips is that it's a strong indicator (validated by a large userbase/adoption) that tooltips aren't needed on touch and that alternative options should be investigated.

That said: the tooltip's open state can be controlled, so you could implement long press functionality externally if you really need it. I would first try to implement a UI on touch devices that avoids needing tooltips at all (since it's the most cross-platform supported behavior), and then implement a long press workaround as a last resort.

A simple mental model is: a popover's trigger's purpose is to open the popover itself (and every platform is supported), while a tooltip's trigger's purpose is unrelated to the tooltip (and touch platforms are excluded).

This makes me think of two related discussions:

- https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/559#issuecomment-2614126253. There, examples of UIs that utilize Radix Tooltip or Material UI Tooltip but should instead use Base UI Popover. Since I never install the mobile app of B2B services, nor use their web mobile version (because it's never great), and instead always lean on their web desktop version on my phone, if any of them use a Base UI Tooltip to show new information that's **not already available** on the screen, then I'm in for a painful UX on mobile. 
- https://www.reddit.com/r/reactjs/comments/1ozo1th/tooltip_components_should_not_exist/, which argues that developers should very rarely need a Tooltip.
*(It also points out that with React Aria, a tooltip must have `<Focusable>` as a child to show up, enforcing that the trigger will always be keyboard tabbable. It also explains why using `title` is a shitshow, e.g. https://mrwweb.com/the-title-attribute-and-why-its-almost-useless/, asking Gemini is actually even better, this could be great to have as an accessibility h2 section in our Tooltip docs)*.

Overall, it gives the vibe that https://base-ui.com/react/components/tooltip#usage-guidelines should be even more strict about valid use cases for the Tooltip, it's niche.

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@1195
```

4186.title: [navigation menu] Keyboard navigation gets into broken state with focus handlers
4186.body:
# Bug report

## Current behavior

The Navigation Menu's component, and demo, assume that a user will never want the top-level menu items to also act as links. By making this assumption, a user navigating the menu with a keyboard can open the menu by putting a menu item into focus and then activating it. However, when the top-level menu item also acts as a link, activating it will navigate the user to the `href` (it will also open the menu, but the navigation away breaks this flow).

Understandably, there is legitimate use cases for both flows. The flow that I need to implement requires that the top-level menu items are links, but the navigation menu is usable via keyboard navigation. To that end, I simply took control of the component's state via `onValueChange/value` and added `onFocus` handlers to the `Trigger`s. This allows someone using a keyboard to tab to a top-level menu item, have the menu open, and then continue to tab down through the submenu's options, ultimately ending up on the next top-level item.

However, something get's out of sync with the tab indices as a user tabs through the component in this way. If they tab all the way through the menu, and then `SHIFT+tab` to go back through, focus ends up in places I would not expect. I'll jump back to a top-level item, then to the first child of the previous top-level item's submenu, then up to it's parent. Then going forward with tabs again skips the top-level items completely and just tabs through the menus because focus is trapped on the first item.

This behavior is not accessible at all and has been very frustrating to try and overcome. Due to the nature of Base UI acting as primitives, I should be able to control the behavior in a more predictable way.

## Expected behavior

I should be able to tab and SHIFT+tab completely through a Navigation Menu in the order of available tabbable items.

## Reproducible example

StackBlitz example: https://stackblitz.com/edit/ztds4r7y?file=src%2FApp.tsx

1. Put focus into the preview and press `tab` to highligh "Overview."
2. Press `tab` until GitHub is highlighed.
3. Press `SHIFT+tab`.
   - Sometimes "Handbook" is focused -- I would expect this to be true.
   - Sometimes "Styling" within the "Handbook" menu is focused.
4. Press `SHIFT+tab`.
   - "Quick Start" within the "Overview" menu is focused -- I would expect "Overview" to be focused.
6. Keep pressing `SHIFT+tab` until "Overview" is focused again.
7. Now start `tab`ing through again until you tab through all submenu items for "Overview."
8. Notice that "Styling" is focused when you would expect "Handbook" to be focused.

## Base UI version

1.2.0

## Which browser are you using?

All

## Which OS are you using?

Mac OS

## Which assistive tech are you using (if applicable)?

Keyboard

## Additional context

Radix's Navigation Menu allows this behavior to work exactly as intended. Reluctantly, I swapped Base UI's NavigationMenu for Radix's because the functionality is the most important part of the component. Because Radix is in maintenance mode, and because Base UI's composition of the component makes it easier to animate and polish, and because I am using Base UI's primitive for nearly every other component in my library, I would prefer to swap back to Base UI once the tab issues are resolved.

nodes.body: BAS-425

Have you considered doing what [Apple's navigation menu](https://www.apple.com/) does instead: It shows a chevron trigger when using the keyboard but otherwise for pointer usage it remains hidden. This is a more accessible pattern than trying to hack on `onFocus` handlers. The `onFocus` technique to support a top-level link that's also a trigger has some issues: 1. A link that also triggers a submenu when it's focused mixes interaction models (navigate vs disclose) 2. On-focus opening is disorienting: tabbing across the navbar pops menus open just by landing on items, which is often discouraged (WCAG 3.2.1 "On Focus") especially for big popups like navigation menus 3. Screen reader browse vs focus mismatch: many SR users move a virtual cursor without real focus, so open on focus doesn't always fire and when it does, it can cause noisy/odd announcements 4. Adding `aria-haspopup/expanded` to an `` announces as “link” so “link, expanded” is confusing and implies conflicting primary actions 5. Focus-management edge cases (what you're hitting here): since the navigation menu popup is in a portal it uses focus guards to properly manage focus. In this case it's breaking due to the custom logic

Thank you for your response. The example you shared from Apple is intriguing, as well as your reasonings for why my proposed pattern might be more burdensome than helpful.

I would be interesting in learning how reimplementing Apple's strategy would work with the primitives from Base UI's NavigationMenu. It would seem that the link would still need to be wrapped fully in the `Trigger`, unless it wasn't and `onMouseEnter/onMouseLeave` handlers fired to managed the state. Additonally, just the arrow couldn't be a `Trigger` if you want the menu `Positioner` to `align="start"` with the entire item.

I will mess around with this some this evening, but I think it would be excellent if this could be documented within Base UI's NavigationMenu. Additionally, an example of this kind of approach and even updates to the component itself to make implementing it more straightforward would be greatly appreciated.

If I can get something working that follows the proposed pattern, I will update here.

> It would seem that the link would still need to be wrapped fully in the Trigger, unless it wasn't and onMouseEnter/onMouseLeave handlers fired to managed the state. Additonally, just the arrow couldn't be a Trigger if you want the menu Positioner to align="start" with the entire item.

Yeah, I haven't tried to do what Apple does with the Base UI navigation menu, but it seems like something we'd need to add new parts for to properly support without hacks on top (e.g. `NavigationMenu.TriggerLink`). I also tried to do this externally and it's not easy to add

Here's a [workaround for v1.3.0](https://stackblitz.com/edit/xshwf691) for the top-level link that opens a menu pattern (Apple-style), which requires splitting the trigger props to individual elements

Thank you so much for your example. I was able to use portions of it to make something work in a much more accessible fashion while maintaining our design pattern. Your work on the library, as well as your example, are much appreciated!

@atomiks With the example pattern you provided, I'm seeing the component get into weird states where `pointer-events: none` gets applied to the `body` tag and doesn't clear. I know your example is not an officially supported method of using the component, but I wanted to reach out to see if there was any known way to disable `pointer-events: none` from being applied to the `body` tag when a sub-menu is opened. I was looking for something like the `modal` prop used on Menus, or something similar, but I haven't found a lead that works.

Just to give some more context, it seems like there is a slight race condition when a menu is open/closing, and a route change is happening via Next router in our case. The soft-nav causes the `pointer-events` to stay on the `body` and not get cleared when a nav happens to happen at the exactly right time. It's very finicky.

I'll have to debug why that happens with the pattern

For now you should be able to set `pointer-events: initial; !important` on `<body>` in your CSS? Regular Menu submenus should no longer need it placed there

4008.title: Why is there no Button Group when you have a Checkbox Group?
4008.body: Good evening, Deaf programmer from New Zealand here, checking out your library for first time. I'm impressed ... Just curious, why is there no ButtonGroup component when there is one for Checkboxes? Curious here ... PS: I wanted to post this under discussions, but couldn't find a button to start a discussion. Perhaps deactivated on your side?

nodes.body: We have a [toggle group](https://base-ui.com/react/components/toggle-group), is that what you're looking for? @binarykitchen

Ah yes, that.

I come from a MUI world and there it's called ButtonGroup:
https://mui.com/material-ui/react-button-group/

But they also provide a ToogleGroup there. A bit confusing 😅

The reason is that "button group" (non-toggles) is just a `role="group"` around some normal buttons, so there isn't anything that a headless lib could add

ToggleGroup has toggle buttons, but also roving focus (keyboard nav). You could also make a "button group" with the [`Toolbar`](https://base-ui.com/react/components/toolbar) component, which supports regular buttons as well as toggle buttons.

Ah, interesting, we are getting closer. Yep, toolbar might be what I'm after. Although I'm a bit but unsure which Toolbar exports to use. `Toolbar.Root`, `Toolbar.Button` or `Button`? In other words, here's a real example: How would you implement those "Resume" and "Stop" button using Base-UI if I may ask?

Since it's only 2 buttons (2 tab stops) roving focus doesn't seem necessary, they could be `<Button>` components (possibly in a generic `div role="group"`)

If the buttons are disabled depending on the playback status (e.g. disable "Resume" when playing) the [focusableWhenDisabled](https://base-ui.com/react/components/button#Button-focusableWhenDisabled) prop can be used so maintain tab order and keep the button discoverable by SRs

Great input @mj12albert - I think I'll go for this.

Last question: it's a component library, unstyled, although I'd like to have some "natural" spacings between those two buttons without the need for styling and CSS (so that I can test it on Storybook).

Is there some component providing spaces between buttons or a native HTML tag which does this?

Thanks for your feedback.

> Is there some component providing spaces between buttons

You could probably make something like Material UI's [Stack component](https://mui.com/material-ui/react-stack/#direction) (or some libraries just call it `<Flex>`)

Thanks, but I'd rather not mix base-ui with Material UI.
This to keep it unstyled while there is still a gap between those two buttons.

In other words, is there some native HTML markup providing some gaps between HTML elements like buttons without CSS?

1930.title: Nested popover closes immediately when using portals
1930.body:
# Bug report

## Current behavior

When using a custom portal container, nested popovers do not work. Clicking the nested popover trigger shows the nested popover for a fraction of a second, then all popovers are closed.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c41e05aa-e5fa-4286-a4fa-d57edbc12c96

## Expected behavior

Nested popovers work, like they do when not using a custom portal container:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f89ad165-b8e8-4873-9658-92d2261d79bf

## Reproducible example

https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/intelligent-taussig-sx9g38?file=%2Fsrc%2FApp.tsx%3A6%2C32

## Base UI version

1.0.0-alpha.8

## Which browser are you using?

Chrome

## Which OS are you using?

Mac OS

## Which assistive tech are you using (if applicable)?

N/A

## Additional context

I am using portals to promote floating UI to the top layer as described here: https://github.com/floating-ui/floating-ui/issues/2931

nodes.body:
You only need to specify the container on the root `Popover.Portal`. The nested one gets appended to the portal of its parent so it stays within its DOM tree.

```jsx
<Popover.Root>
  <Popover.Trigger>Open</Popover.Trigger>
  <Popover.Portal container={dialogNode}>
    <Popover.Positioner>
      <Popover.Popup>
        Popover
        <Popover.Root>
          <Popover.Trigger>Open nested</Popover.Trigger>
          {/* remove container={dialogNode} */}
          <Popover.Portal container={dialogNode}>
            <Popover.Positioner>
              <Popover.Popup>Nested popover</Popover.Popup>
            </Popover.Positioner>
          </Popover.Portal>
        </Popover.Root>
      </Popover.Popup>
    </Popover.Positioner>
  </Popover.Portal>
</Popover.Root>
```

> Context is provided so that portals nested in one another are appended to their respective parent.

https://floating-ui.com/docs/FloatingPortal

This is tricky because we're trying to do it generically without having to pass the portal container around everywhere. E.g. given some component `MyPopover` I would like to be able to render it anywhere in the component tree and have the portals just work based on the surrounding context, without the need for conditionally drilling the portal container prop based on the level of nesting.

Here's an example: https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/gifted-ishizaka-n7ygcg?file=%2Fsrc%2FApp.tsx%3A11%2C13&workspaceId=ws_4qKVDKGuRrrvd8oLfoRWSz

At that point, it seems a lot easier to use Base UI's Dialog than the native one. The native one has more downsides trying to interop with everything than not (3rd party extensions, other popups, etc) that I don't see benefit to using it.

Yeah, you might be right. It just seems like a step backwards, because we've been using native modal dialogs in production for awhile now and it's been (mostly) fine. 😅

(Thanks for all your help btw!)
I have a solution to this (in a transitive dependency) https://github.com/floating-ui/floating-ui/pull/3318

I might be looking at this too simplistically but if you know which popover is 'attached' to the dialog, which I'd assume you do, can you pass in a bool that says this is the only one that need to be attached to the dialog?

https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/nested-popup-in-dialog-j8nzmg

> if you know which popover is 'attached' to the dialog, which I'd assume you do

Unfortunately we don't. In our application, any route can be rendered both outside or inside a modal, so it's not as straightforward as passing a prop to the outermost popover.

I did however come up with this idea that would work in our case: clear the portal context as soon as we pass the value to Base UI. https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/agitated-ptolemy-ngpn2n?file=%2Fsrc%2FApp.tsx%3A23%2C3&workspaceId=ws_4qKVDKGuRrrvd8oLfoRWSz
This is fixed in `beta.0`

3693.title: [dialog] Don't add invisible span element after clicked trigger
3693.body: # Bug report ## Current behavior This is not necessarily a bug report, but rather unwanted behavior. After clicking a Trigger, Base UI will create a new `span` element directly after the button. As I understand it from *very* briefly looking at the source, this has something to do with trapping the focus? ```html ``` In a striped list of buttons however, this has the result that what is `even` and `odd` shifts around when opening and closing the dialog. I'm sure there are other scenarios where adding a span element as a sibling is unwanted. ## Expected behavior Opening a Dialog does not create a hidden span element behind the trigger. ## Reproducible example [All docs examples](https://base-ui.com/react/components/dialog#controlled-mode-with-multiple-triggers) ## Base UI version v1.0.0 ## Which browser are you using? Edge 143 ## Which OS are you using? Windows 11 ## Which assistive tech are you using (if applicable)? N/A ## Additional context N/A

nodes.body:
> this has something to do with trapping the focus?

Looks like that span is a fallback element for resolving where to return focus: https://github.com/mui/base-ui/blob/1845fc60932c4ae2a0848550c6d3e58131f7e483/packages/react/src/floating-ui-react/components/FloatingFocusManager.tsx#L783-L792

> what is `even` and `odd` shifts around

You're referring to `:nth-child(odd)`? @Studio384

> > what is `even` and `odd` shifts around
> 
> You're referring to `:nth-child(odd)`? [@Studio384](https://github.com/Studio384)

Yes, sorry, my brain was in Tailwind mode when I wrote that...

While this could be removed for modal dialogs since `finalFocus` solves this better (manually), it can't be changed for non-modal dialogs or popovers. Any CSS selectors that rely on the presence of DOM elements are affected by these hidden elements (focus guards). 

You should as a workaround be able to wrap each trigger inside a parent `<div>` which will contain all the elements and let the parent still see the presence of one single element.

Another issue of having these span elements is the UI is affected, specifically the width of the parent element increases.

I am using shadcn/ui and its [Base UI based dropdown-menu](https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui/blob/main/apps/v4/registry/bases/base/ui/dropdown-menu.tsx) uses Menu. The dropdown-menu is then used in the [data table column header](https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui/blob/18bd8f07cba4e71e3d7e4c8d738b90c30d3717b9/apps/v4/app/(app)/examples/tasks/components/data-table-column-header.tsx#L32) to sort the columns. After migrating my code from Radix UI to Base UI, I noticed that when the column header is clicked to select the sorting order, the width of the column increased while the popup is opened due to the added span element.

I found a similar report https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/3166 but the behavior above still happens with v1.1.0. Is there a workaround to disable the span element? Maybe set `finalFocus` to `false`?

The focus guards/hidden elements exist for a11y purposes and can't just be removed. Radix's (modal) implementation wasn't accessible to touch screen readers so it could avoid the hidden elements but we ensure you can tab/move out of the menu using a screen reader

> the width of the column increased while the popup is opened due to the added span element.

Have you tried wrapping a `<div>` around the trigger? In general, you'll need to ensure you aren't relying on the presence of DOM elements for the CSS so the hidden elements don't affect your layout. All the elements are visually hidden so they don't take up size themselves as they set `position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0`. If they're affecting the layout, it's because a parent CSS selector is doing something like `:nth-child()`

Thanks @atomiks , [wrapping the trigger with `<div>`](https://github.com/CHC383/baseui-table-menu/blob/0b9b2b9ca55143ab3432765f70c0a3f89f29548d/src/components/data-table/data-table-column-header.tsx#L39) doesn't resolve the issue. Please find the reproduction in https://github.com/CHC383/baseui-table-menu (instructions in README)

Thanks for the repro. This is the DOM presence selector that causes the issue: `space-x-2` You're right that wrapping with `
` doesn't work, because several more of the elements get rendered outside of it, as they're part of the `Portal` element (or `Content` in shadcn). Wrapping it around both the trigger and content works though (or just the whole `
... ...
```
Thanks for the insights, and I think `space-x-2` (and `flex items-center`) is not needed as the only visible element is the trigger, there is really nothing to layout.

3950.title: Adding data-base-ui-inert to DOM elements when floating elements open makes component usage restrictive and unpredictable
3950.body:
# Get help

## Ask a question

I have been using base-ui with tiptap/prosemirror library. It tracks the content editable area for mutations and sync or recalculate it's state if DOM changes.

If I am using nodeView or reactNodeView to render anything inside this area then I need to be very careful of where I place my Popover/Menu trigger or portal as they will add data-base-ui-inert to all the sibling which will trigger the resync with prosemirror

Having these components change the DOM unexpectedly makes it volatile to use as it's a side-effect which may result in unexpected behaviours especially when DOM is being observed by me or any third party library.

There must be a better way to do this, according to my understanding this approach is used to -
1. Trap focus inside Popup and avoid keyboard navigation to select elements outside of Popup area
2. Enable click on an element outside popover area and not register it as outside click if it doesn't have the inert attribute in it's ancestor

Outside click have been solved by hundreds of component libraries, can't those be considered, just want to understand the approach and if there's any fundamental issue that I am missing

Thank you for support!

nodes.body:
> Enable click on an element outside popover area and not register it as outside click if it doesn't have the inert attribute in it's ancestor

Yes, this is the main reason - it's to support 3rd party extension elements so clicking on them won't close the popover as it won't be considered an "outside" click.

In the case of `Dialog`, it also adds `aria-hidden="true"` to every outside element, which is a similar mutation to `data-base-ui-inert`. Does that one not cause problems? Dialog also requires an explicit click on the backdrop if it's `modal`, which Popover doesn't require

But it can easily be opt-in instead of forcing it by default, like instead of checking data-base-ui-inert in ancestors to decide whether to close Popup or not we can have data-base-ui-popup or something like that and not close popup when it's present in ancestor, this way user will be making conscious decision of opting in not to close popup when clicked inside an area outside of popup 

Having data-base-ui-inert on backdrop is not an issue as it's an element added by Popover itself

Problem arises only when popover mutates elements in DOM unrelated to Popover/Menu, and yes attributes like aria-hidden="true" also causes problem. Mutation on root elements like body for scroll lock does not cause problem as they are rarely observed but since it mutates almost all sibling elements it cause issues

Appreciate the response, Thanks!

`data-base-ui-inert` marks every current outside element at mount time, so popups from extensions injected after (the typical case) don't get marked and so don't close when pressed. The alternative is storing every "outside" element in memory and comparing by reference instead of the attr which is more expensive

With your suggestion of `data-base-ui-popup`, the extension element isn't controlled by you, so it can't be marked by your code (easily) to block outside clicks on it

I understand the use-case, will keep it in mind. Will share details if I hit a wall while working with libraries that often depends on DOM mutations to work like prosemirror's content editable area

Thanks again!

One solution is a prop of elements that you can pass (e.g. the prosemirror container) to prevent `data-base-ui-inert` from getting set on anything inside of it, or the attrs to instead mark the highest possible ancestor

That would solve my immediate issue, but I can also solve it by placing both popover trigger and popup outside of prosemirror container, would be hassle but it'll achieve the same output

Ideally I would expect it to not mutate unrelated DOM at all, but there must have been a reason to use this approach by base-ui team, at least that's what I expect

I think adding it in docs to let consumer know about this side-effect would be better for now

@ziyadshaikh013 try out the version in #3955 

```
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@3955
```

It mutates the highest level node (app element root/before `<body>`), which avoids issues with editors that watch for node mutations while supporting 3rd party extension elements. It's assumed they append their popups to `<body>` outside of the app tree

The issue I encounter is that base-ui **Dialog** taks a long time to appear under the following conditions: 1. The ProseMirror editor contains a large number of nodes (several thousands or more). 2. At least one of those nodes renders an element with an `aria-live` attribute somewhere in its DOM subtree. ``` Like this:

... ``` > [!NOTE] > A real use case is embedding CodeMirror as an editable code block in ProseMirror. CodeMirror's DOM tree contains an element with `aria-live="polite"` attribute. ([source](https://github.com/codemirror/view/blob/038f0ae53b69f6759d22393dbe984b969d38ba82/src/editorview.ts#L188)) ## Reproduction https://stackblitz.com/edit/base-ui-dialog-perf-issue?file=src%2FApp.tsx The reproduction project demonstrates using **Dialog** to implement a global quick search popup that opens with the Ctrl/Cmd + P keyboard shortcut. When the dialog opens, I observed the following behavior: * base-ui adds `data-base-ui-inert` and `aria-hidden="true"` to all elements inside the ProseMirror `

` container. * This triggers the [`MutationObserver`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/MutationObserver) callback in [`prosemirror-view`](https://github.com/ProseMirror/prosemirror-view/blob/9df3a26f4518f6dfd7fb00db775f93916b2d8110/src/domobserver.ts#L53). * As a result, ProseMirror re-syncs the DOM with its internal document model. * ProseMirror removes those attributes because they don't exist in the document model. The re-sync process takes roughly **150× longer** than the time base-ui spends setting those DOM attributes, which causes the noticeable delay when opening the dialog. > [!NOTE] > ProseMirror re-syncing 3000 paragraphs: > > > > base-ui setting attributes on 3000 paragraphs: > >

981.title: [slider] Incorrect pointer tracking when padding is applied to the "poles" of the Control
981.body:
- We need a prop or a CSS variable to make a slider to work like Radix/macOS slider—Radix and macOS sliders position the Thumb so that its edges are contained within the track (and we always align the Thumb's center to the track edge instead, so it ends up poking out of the Track at the edges)
- `minStepsBetweenValues` name wasn't too intuitive—will think if there's something better
- Cursor movement is tracked incorrectly when the Control part has padding

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/16a1bf2e-2ead-4555-8b65-167653920706

Not sure what Control sizing has to do anything with the cursor movement; I'd expect it all to be done relative to the Track

### Done

- ✅ ~I expected divs instead of spans, would have saved me a couple situations when my width/height styles wouldn't work right away~ https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1017
- ✅ ~Indicator has position absolute, which makes it harder to style. I don't understand how this is helping me. See [this PR](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/980/files#diff-330f4fceb57c701e55a3f3f00a53cdad1f2505a40c351e5e49263c810cb30b25) for an example.~ https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1017
  - ✅ ~Note: when closing this issue, please make sure that [this](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/blob/master/docs/src/app/(public)/(content)/react/components/slider/demos/hero/tailwind/index.tsx#L9-L10) and [this](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/blob/master/docs/src/app/(public)/(content)/react/components/slider/demos/hero/css-modules/index.module.css#L19-L20) kludge in the demos are removed.~  https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1054
- ✅ ~Thumb has position absolute, which makes sense. However, if it's positioned absolutely, we need to specify relative to what, otherwise it's a gotcha for the dev to figure out and find the Thumb in a random position on the page first. We need to set `position: relative` on the Track.~ https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1017

**Search keywords**:

nodes.body:
> I'd expect it all to be done relative to the Track

Movements are actually relative to the `Control` which is the "tracked area", and the `Track` is just a dumb component purely for styling the track, because without it you'd have to use a pseudo-element on the `Control` to style a "visible track" (or insert your own span)... naming issue?

> Cursor movement is tracked incorrectly when the Control part has padding

This is probably still a bug, I'll check it out

> Movements are actually relative to the `Control` which is the "tracked area", and the `Track` is just a dumb component purely for styling the track, because without it you'd have to use a pseudo-element on the `Control` to style a "visible track" (or insert your own span)... naming issue? I was explained that `Control` is the clickable area, so I had used a bit of padding on it (including horizontal) to make clicks and taps easier. I think no matter what size the Control is, the Thumb would always be placed relative to the visible Track. In the Anatomy we are also placing Thumb into the Track so altogether that's why I expected it to work this way

I'll remove the horizontal padding from Control in my demo just to be safe for now, but let's still take a note to discuss later whether it's a legit design decision

https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/980/commits/de8e9ab72c10a437856c270b2a2463aa90b4583d

> We need a prop or a CSS variable to make a slider to work like Radix/macOS slider

@vladmoroz "Need" seems a bit strong here? We designed it this way intentionally. When the thumb is contained, the thumb position usually doesn't visually communicate the value accurately, and the pointer becomes misaligned. The current approach seems like a better design?

@colmtuite I'd actually argue that inset thumb has to be the default > the thumb position usually doesn't visually communicate the value accurately Slider isn't a component for entering precise values anyway, so this doesn't seem like the most important constraint here. Sometimes it's important, sure—I'm well aware of https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives/issues/1966, but precise mapping is more of an edge case for slider in my opinion. It's fine as long as it's going to be an option. Otherwise, we can't ignore a class of slider designs, like this one:

Centered thumb wouldn't work in UIs like this one too because it would yield a layout collision:

iOS sliders are the same. Because of potential layout collisions and misalignments, I'd almost never want to use a centered thumb—I wouldn't use a slider when a precise value matters anyway. > the pointer becomes misaligned This has always seemed just like an implementation mistake in Radix. Don't see why it should be misaligned—if thumb has, say, 100px of travel, that should be covered by 100px of pointer travel. Native range input sliders (which are inset by default) and macOS sliders don't have this issue: https://jsfiddle.net/1j9x06yu/ *** One more point to consider is that with Radix, it's possible to implement a centered thumb yourself via a 0px thumb element and pseudo elements for the visual thumb, but the reverse doesn't work—you can't implement an inset thumb with the design that we have. *** Altogether, I think we need something like `position: "inset" | "centered"` on the `Thumb`, with `inset` being the default because it slots into any design layout well and can work with two most common slider design styles—a thin track with a larger thumb, and a thick track and same size thumb.

> Thumb has position absolute, which makes sense. However, if it's positioned absolutely, we need to specify relative to what, otherwise it's a gotcha for the dev to figure out and find the Thumb in a random position on the page first. We need to set position: relative on the Track.

Yes, if we're setting `position: absolute` as an inline style, then we must set `position: relative` as an inline style too. Either we set both inline styles, or leave both up to the user.

> Indicator has position absolute, which makes it harder to style. I don't understand how this is helping me.

Hmm right yeah, it's nested inside the track so it just needs a way to handle width

> Either we set both inline styles, or leave both up to the user.

How about for all the slider parts we leave the `position` property up to the user? `position` is relatively easy to figure out

> `Control` is the clickable area, so I had used a bit of padding on it (including horizontal) to make clicks and taps easier.

As a workaround, invisible pseudo-elements can be used pad the "poles" of the control to make the hitbox bigger, here's a sandbox (where they have a red background): https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/misty-hooks-2f9qgd?file=%2Fsrc%2Findex.module.css

Taps/clicks would immediately set the value to the min or max, is that how it should be expected to work if padding is applied (on all sides)?

Sometimes it's important, sure—I'm well aware of [radix-ui/primitives#1966](https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives/issues/1966), but precise mapping is more of an edge case for slider in my opinion. It's fine as long as it's going to be an option. > > Otherwise, we can't ignore a class of slider designs, like this one: > > > > Centered thumb wouldn't work in UIs like this one too because it would yield a layout collision: > > > > iOS sliders are the same. Because of potential layout collisions and misalignments, I'd almost never want to use a centered thumb—I wouldn't use a slider when a precise value matters anyway. > > > the pointer becomes misaligned > > This has always seemed just like an implementation mistake in Radix. Don't see why it should be misaligned—if thumb has, say, 100px of travel, that should be covered by 100px of pointer travel. > > Native range input sliders (which are inset by default) and macOS sliders don't have this issue: > > https://jsfiddle.net/1j9x06yu/ > > One more point to consider is that with Radix, it's possible to implement a centered thumb yourself via a 0px thumb element and pseudo elements for the visual thumb, but the reverse doesn't work—you can't implement an inset thumb with the design that we have. > > Altogether, I think we need something like `position: "inset" | "centered"` on the `Thumb`, with `inset` being the default because it slots into any design layout well and can work with two most common slider design styles—a thin track with a larger thumb, and a thick track and same size thumb.

Was able to make a somewhat decent inset slider using the current API: https://deploy-preview-1609--base-ui.netlify.app/experiments/slider/inset

- put `Control` inside `Track` (opposite of the documented anatomy)
- use semi-circular pseudo-elements to extend the `Control`'s hitbox
- to put a border around the whole thing, use `box-shadow` and add semi-circular pseudo-elements to the `Track` for the shadow to wrap around (~~there is a 1px overlap I haven't solved yet though~~ fixed with `clip-path: inset(…)`)

I haven't thoroughly tested if the pseudo-elements on control would cause any bugs or inaccuracies in the values yet

CC @vladmoroz
@mj12albert The functionality works great - is there a way we could keep the original design (with the circle thumb bigger than the track bar) with the inset slider?

> is there a way we could keep the original design (with the circle thumb bigger than the track bar

Yep the sizes can be changed accordingly, I added another [example](https://deploy-preview-1609--base-ui.netlify.app/experiments/slider/inset) @raclee7

After https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1661 it may be possible to round the poles of the Control and Track using `border-radius` alone (instead of using pseudo-elements) since pointer events outside the border-radius curve do not run

https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1609/commits/85b1e6761cc9a80c57a37746d07f6e0794b033d2

The Control/Track do not have to be reversed anymore, and only one pseudo-element is needed to extend the Indicator towards the "min" end

We decided to support this with a prop - server-rendering this requires knowing the computed width of the thumb(s), will try to use Tab's `renderBeforeHydration` method: https://base-ui.com/react/components/tabs#TabsIndicator-renderBeforeHydration

This feature will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

4122.title: Radio.Root, Checkbox.Root, and Switch.Root do not receive an accessible name from a wrapping <label> element
4122.body: # Bug report ## Current behavior Wrapping `Radio.Root`, `Checkbox.Root`, or `Switch.Root` inside a native HTML `

nodes.body: BAS-399

We noticed this in https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/3205#issuecomment-3540347376 and believe it to be an axe issue

I've only tested it with Mac voiceover though, did you find this announced incorrectly on a screen reader? @hsimpson270

@mj12albert the screen reader was a good idea. I tested using Windows Narrator and can confirm the checkbox label does not get read out using just the label wrapper without also assigning the id. Once the id is assigned to the `label` and then referenced using `aria-labelledby` then Narrator picks it up and reads it out correctly. So based on that it appears to be an actual issue and not just an axe specific one.

The reason these components (Checkbox, Radio, Switch) render a `<span>` is because otherwise an enclosing `<label>` creates invalid HTML since it wraps two form controls:

```jsx
<label>
  <Checkbox.Root />
</label>
```
Creates this:
```html
<label>
  <button />
  <input />
</label>
```

The alternative (without using `aria-labelledby`) is to render a `<button>` like this:

```jsx
<Checkbox.Root 
  nativeButton
  render={(props) => (
    <label>
      <button {...props} />
      {label}
    </label>
  )}
/>
```

(Side note: The `<Field.Root>` component sets `aria-labelledby` automatically, so it also works without needing to change the tag or use `render`.)

An alternative API is making additional parts for this component:

```jsx
<Checkbox.Root>
  <Checkbox.Trigger> {/* <button /> */}
    <Checkbox.Indicator />
  </Checkbox.Trigger>
</Checkbox.Root>
```

I can confirm this is a WCAG compliance issue with **high severity** and has no workaround. This is not just an axe-core issue. You can inspect in the browser and see that the inputs have no accessible name in the Accessibility Tree: ![The Radio component example with devtools open on the side showing the accessibility tree with an unlabelled radio highlighted](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/56bfd416-2fd1-4d17-affd-f84bfc39e1f8) And you can see the same result in the accessibility panel: ![The accessibility panel in Chrome devtools highlighting the element with a role of radio is unnamed](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/eaaeca95-6882-4fbe-a76e-666feb56f4b2) While JAWS and VoiceOver will use nearby text to take a "best guess" at the intended label, other screen readers will not do this kind of auto-correction. As a result, checkboxes, radios and switches are all announced as un-named controls by: * NVDA * Windows Narrator * Android Talkback The most common accessible implementations of these types of components are to 1. Use the default input/button (i.e. not make it aria-hidden) but make it visually-hidden (`.sr-only`) so that it remains accessible, and then use CSS to style adjacent elements to _look like_ the intended control. 2. OR use `aria-label` (or aria-labelledby) on the custom control, along with all additional keyboard behaviour The first option is considerably simpler, as it doesn't involve re-creating default HTML elements. I'm unclear on why a `span` is being used with a changed `role` for elements that already exist in HTML. If it is essential for some reason, then these components will all need their labels to be explicitly linked in the code. For example: ```html ``` The affected components are: 1. Checkbox 2. Checkbox Group 3. Radio 4. Radio Group 5. Switch** **Note: The switch component is also affected in its current implementation, as it is using a checkbox role. But this isn't the best role or structure for an ON/OFF switch, which I will raise separately. The switch should be a button with `aria-pressed` attribute and not a checkbox. It will still need an accessible name to be programmatically associated with it.

@grace-snow yep you're correct: I went and tested NVDA, Narrator, and JAWS and found the same thing. 

We're fixing it in #4142 so `aria-labelledby` is added to the `<span>` control to correctly link the accessible name. There are no API changes required for this to start working in the next version.

> I can confirm this is a WCAG compliance issue with high severity **and has no workaround**. This is not just an axe-core issue.

It does have several workarounds in the meantime (until that gets merged and released):

1. Render a `<button>` instead of a `<span>`:
```jsx
<Checkbox.Root 
  nativeButton
  render={(props) => (
    <label>
      <button {...props} />
      {label}
    </label>
  )}
/>
```

2. Use `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`:

```jsx
<label>
  <Checkbox.Root aria-label={label} />
  {label}
</label>
```

---

> **Note: The switch component is also affected in its current implementation, as it is using a checkbox role. But this isn't the best role or structure for an ON/OFF switch, which I will raise separately. The switch should be a button with aria-pressed attribute and not a checkbox. It will still need an accessible name to be programmatically associated with it.

The visible control (that gets focus) has `role="switch"` + `aria-checked`, which matches the pattern [correctly](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Reference/Roles/switch_role). The hidden input (just for forms) uses `type="checkbox"` but that's not exposed to screen readers

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@4142
```

nodes.body:
Thanks for the detailed request @Stusu  — I try to match a common pattern (PrimeReact-style “select all visible”).

I’m working on this in https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/4962 and started with Combobox.SelectAll, which:

Works in multiple modes (controlled and uncontrolled)
Toggles all currently filtered / visible items (respects the combobox filter)
Exposes checked, indeterminate, and disabled for styling (data-checked, data-indeterminate, etc.)
Uses `role="checkbox"` and `aria-checked` for accessibility
Proposed usage  — place it above the list inside the popup (pinned header row):

```
<Combobox.Root items={items} multiple>
  {/* ... */}
  <Combobox.Popup>
    <Combobox.SelectAll />
    <Combobox.List>
      {(item) => (
        <Combobox.Item value={item}>{item.label}</Combobox.Item>
      )}
    </Combobox.List>
  </Combobox.Popup>
</Combobox.Root>
```

I didn’t add `Combobox.ListHeade`r yet, to keep the PR smaller. A plain wrapper (<div>) or your own styled row above <Combobox.List> should be enough to pin the control. If you still want a first-class ListHeader inside <Combobox.List> (as in your example), that’s a good follow-up — happy to discuss whether it should be layout-only or tied to list semantics (keyboard nav, virtualized lists, etc.).

@Stusu You can achieve this in userland: https://stackblitz.com/edit/cthmvuwz?file=src%2FApp.tsx

`Combobox.List` is a real `role="listbox"` so it can't contain anything other than real options, so we need to hack a `Combobox.Item` for this. 

Not sure if we could have actual support for this as it would deviate from the ARIA combobox pattern though. The closest is this footnote from listbox:
> If selecting or unselecting all options is an important function, implementing separate controls for these actions, such as buttons for "Select All" and "Unselect All", significantly improves accessibility.

@soheilnikroo it's better to have some common terms in the discussion before opening a PR that might not get reviewed/merged. It might seem like a minor thing, but increasing the API surface of a Base UI component is a big deal; we don't do that slightly.

In your [PR](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/4962), the newly added `Combobox.SelectAll` checkbox is not accessible.

Thanks @aarongarciah — fair points on both counts.

I should have discussed this on the issue before opening a PR; I’m still learning Base UI’s process and didn’t treat the API surface with the care it deserves.

I’ll close the PR unless you’d rather I narrow it to docs for the userland `Item` approach. Sorry for the noise, and thanks for the accessibility callout — happy to learn what specifically didn’t work in your testing if you can share.

> what specifically didn’t work

The main issue is that keyboard navigation will not reach it, hence the reason for hacking `Combobox.Item` ~

Thanks @aarongarciah — that makes sense, and sorry for opening the PR before aligning on the approach.

You’re right: keyboard navigation couldn’t reach the original `Combobox.SelectAll` because it was a separate `role="checkbox"` control outside the `role="listbox"`. I’ve updated the PR so `Combobox.SelectAll` is implemented as a `Combobox.Item` (same idea as the Stackblitz `Item` hack), placed as the first child inside `Combobox.List`, with `preventBaseUIHandler()` so the sentinel value isn’t added to the selection. Arrow keys + Enter now reach it, and there’s a test for that.

I also updated `Combobox.List` to allow a static `<Combobox.SelectAll />` sibling before the collection render function.

3630.title: [combobox] Allow Combobox.Clear to be focusable and keyboard navigatable
3630.body:
# Feature request

Allow the "Clear selection" button to be accessible by keyboard navigation (tab and arrow keys)

## Summary

Currently `Combobox.Clear` renders with `tabIndex={-1}` which makes it inaccessible, it would be great if the component has a prop that allows the users to access it via keyboard

## Examples in other libraries

https://www.mongodb.design/component/combobox/live-example

## Motivation

Currently, keyboard-only users cannot clear their selections without using a mouse, creating a significant accessibility barrier.

Current workaround limitations:
- Users must either use a mouse (not possible for some users)
- Delete selections one by one (time-consuming for multiple selections)
- Navigate away and return to reset the field (disrupts workflow)

nodes.body:
The cleara button is a mouse equivalent of the <kbd>Del</kbd> keyboard key, so there is no need to be focusable inside it will create more friction as each selected item will need to either act as a trap focus zone or have two tab stops. 

So, in practice, Arrow left/right allow keyboard user to focus each combobox item that is selected, and Del removed the item.

I agree that two focusable elements will create friction for the `Combobox.Chip` component,

but this request is for the  `Combobox.Clear` component not `Combobox.ChipRemove`

`<Combobox.Clear tabIndex={0}>` works, no? But yes, the problem here is multiple tab stops in a form. Ideally each field is one single tab stop. For keyboard users (when not using a screen reader), <kbd>Esc</kbd> will clear the value in the input (when the popup is closed), or deleting all the text in the input
@atomiks Yes <kbd>Esc</kbd> works, but when you are in a `Dialog`, <kbd>Esc</kbd> would actually close the dialog as well. Atleast thats what happened when I used it in a Radix `Dialog`.

It does prevent bubbling on <kbd>Esc</kbd>, and works with our Dialog at least

> keyboard navigation with left and right arrow keys is done by the library and won't focus the Clear component

What is the reason the Clear button should be part of the composite loop? Other examples of this pattern (e.g. Chakra UI and Material UI) don't include the clear button as part of the focus loop or tab order

A blog post https://scottaohara.github.io/clear-text-field-button/ explains why the clear button is not in the tab order:

> The clear button is purposefully not in the tabbing order of the web page. Keyboard users can use the Delete or Backspace keys (or first select all the text field's text and then use these keys) to delete the contents of the text field.
> 
> Important: if the clear button was part of the web page's tabbing order, every text field a user had entered data into would then require that they tab past the clear button that they have no use for. This clear button being useless to them if they had filled out the field with the data they intended. Adding clear buttons to the web page's tabbing order is not recommended for this reason, as it has the potential to require two keyboard focus stops for every text field on the page that has a clear button.



> The HTML <input type="search"> also excludes the clear button from the tab sequence https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/input/search.

https://github.com/mui/material-ui/issues/44936#issuecomment-2604768121

It's interesting that the input with type="search" works in this way, though this was introduced in 2015 and seems outdated.

IMO, I don't agree with the blog post, and I think this is not great UX. There isn't a way for the screen reader to announce the clear button, and there IS obviously a button which is intentionally hidden from screen readers (i.e., people who rely on screen readers).

Hiding it from screen reader, on `blur` or on `mouseout` just makes it less discoverable.

Also, MDN states this: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/Guides/Understanding_WCAG/Keyboard#click%E2%80%A6 (Currently the `Combobox.Clear` is a button → clickable element, and has `tabIndex={-1}` which removes the interactive semantics)

https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/keyboard.html - You can argue that the `Esc` key is enough to satisfy this constraint, but providing multiple paths is better UX.

@StefanDejanovskiOcuco the screen readers don't need to announce anything about the clear button. They announce it's a combobox. Screen reader users know that a combobox content could be removed when pressing escape. If you take a look at the [ARIA examples](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/combobox/examples/combobox-autocomplete-list/) you won't see any clear button at all. The clear button is just a helper button that has been introduced for mouse users, nothing more. It improves their UX by just clicking the button instead of clicking the input, selecting all content, and pressing delete.

Also, regarding the mongo db example, their UX decision involves a learning curve for users. In their combobox, you could clear the content with Esc, but not after you selected an option, you can only use the X button for that, which only appears after selection. Anyway, it's their UX decision, it's not a standard combobox pattern, which is the one we opt for.

3525.title: [toggle] Single selection mode with role="radio"
3525.body:
Hello team!

Congrats on your first stable release!

My question is around the Toggle/ToggleGroup components in their single selection mode.

As per [this example in shadcn](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/toggle-group#single), Radix `ToggleGroup` sets `role="radio"` to `ToggleGroupItem`'s in single selection mode and I was kind of expecting the same from Base UI `ToggleGroup` when `multiple={false}`.

I see the same behavior in React Spectrum [ToggleGroup](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-aria/ToggleButtonGroup.html#single-selection)

Is there a reason we are not doing the same on the Base UI component?

TIA!

nodes.body:
The Base UI toggle implements the [toggle button pattern](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/), which is “a two-state button that can be either off (not pressed) or on (pressed)”; and what distinguishes it from a regular button or a radio button is that it uses `aria-pressed` to announce the pressed state

And unlike a radio button it isn't a form control and it's value isn't included in form submission

> Radix `ToggleGroup` sets `role="radio"` to `ToggleGroupItem`'s in single selection mode

Radix itself doesn't seem to do this in their [own examples](https://www.radix-ui.com/primitives/docs/components/toggle-group).

Looking at the shadcn and RA examples, their toggle group components are actually plain radio groups (using `<button role="radio">` for the radios that don't have `aria-pressed`) that are styled to visually resemble toggle groups; this seems weird as it would create a mismatch for sighted SR users 🤔

Thank you for your response @mj12albert. > Radix itself doesn't seem to do this in their [own examples](https://www.radix-ui.com/primitives/docs/components/toggle-group). I do see `role="radio"` in Radix's example that you linked -
Oh yeah you're right; it uses `aria-checked` instead of `aria-pressed` (like a radio button should) but the `ARIA design pattern` link also links to the (toggle) button pattern 🤷‍♂️

> Oh yeah you're right; it uses `aria-checked` instead of `aria-pressed` (like a radio button should) but the `ARIA design pattern` link also links to the (toggle) button pattern 🤷‍♂️ 

Thats probably because they still follow the Toggle button pattern for multiple selection mode. And if I am not wrong, its the same with RA. It's only in the single selection mode where the items become radio buttons, and hence my question for Base UI.

Regardless of single/multiple selection, toggle group and radio group are different components; you can press a "selected" toggle to un-press/de-select it, you should not be able to uncheck a checked radio to "clear" it

All these other libraries have both so it seems weird to me to have a radio group and a toggle group that's actually a radio group

> Thats probably because they still follow the Toggle button pattern for multiple selection mode. And if I am not wrong, its the same with RA. It's only in the single selection mode where the items become radio buttons, and hence my question for Base UI.

I think the reason they use `role="radio"` for single select is because otherwise users might not have any indication that the component only allows a single selection. With the radiogroup pattern, single selection is expected and the screen reader announces how many options there are.

Maybe a separate issue, but using `role="toolbar"` instead of `role="group"` for multiple selection further improves the screen reader UX because `role="toolbar"` makes [the keyboard navigation](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/toolbar/#keyboardinteraction) of the component less unexpected.

> With the radiogroup pattern, single selection is expected and the screen reader announces how many options there are.

Why not use the radio group component directly? The problem with toggles with `role="radio"` is that it's wrong to be able to toggle the same "radio" between checked/unchecked

@mj12albert As far as I can tell, the core issue with the Toggle component in single selection mode is that there is no _non-visual indication_ that it only supports a single selection. _One possible solution_ would be to change the role to radiogroup/radio as they are doing in the react-aria component.

719.title: [docs] Which package url is valid ?
719.body:
### Related page

https://mui.com/base-ui/getting-started/quickstart/

### Kind of issue

Other

If we look in the readme and click on the `npm` badge, we see that the npm package is `@base_ui/react`. The latest version for this package is `v1.0.0-alpha3`, which matches the `release` section of this project.
But if we go on the website [mui.com/base-ui/](https://mui.com/base-ui/) tagged in the `about` section of the project, the displayed npm command is `npm install @mui/base`, same in [the docs](https://mui.com/base-ui/getting-started/quickstart/#installation).
Which one is the correct one ?

### Context

**Search keywords**: npm, url, link

nodes.body: I agree that this is confusing and that the current doc of `@mui/base` doc should be clearer on this @samuelsycamore

Thanks for raising this @loudjanilef. I agree that the current state is pretty confusing. The Overview page does include this warning callout:

> Base UI's API is currently being revised; there will be no new features or components added to the current implementation. Learn more about plans for Base UI in [this blog post](https://mui.com/blog/base-ui-2024-plans/).

But this could be a lot more prominent and a lot more clear now that there are two npm packages out there. And it should probably be repeated in the Quickstart guide. 

What if we moved the callout to the top of both pages and revised it like this:

> This documentation describes how to work with the `@mui/base` package. The Base UI API is currently being revised and will soon be released as `@base_ui/react` with a new set of docs. There will be no new features or components added to `@mui/base`, and we will announce when the new package is ready to migrate to. Learn more about plans for Base UI in [this blog post](https://mui.com/blog/base-ui-2024-plans/).

Would that make things more clear? cc @colmtuite

Also, I just realized that the blog post linked in that callout doesn't exactly provide more details on the packages and such. Should we update that post to explain the latest state of things? (Could just be a few sentences at the end to address the confusion described here.)

One could say that the callout should even be on every single page of the `@mui/base` doc
It's very easy to reach a component page like [this one](https://mui.com/base-ui/react-number-input/) with Google and start using `@mui/base` without knowing the consequences:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7b29f3ec-44a4-4ffa-99dc-1bf085b5f047)
We will add a callout once we have the new docs published. We currently don't have anything to point folks to from the old docs.

> We currently don't have anything to point folks to from the old docs.

Still, it would be nice to at least provide more context for folks who may be confused by the two competing npm packages, don't you think?

> Would that make things more clear? cc @colmtuite

I'm not sure, I don't think so? @flaviendelangle is correct that the callout should be on every page, and it will be. But right now, anything can change. For example, the new npm package can (and almost certainly will) change. We're too early to start mentioning the new package or linking people to new docs. So there's nothing to do here at this point.

@loudjanilef To answer your question, there is no "correct one", best to think of them as two different libs. This project is not released yet, and has no docs, so it's not ready to use just yet. Soon though!

cc @samuelsycamore

Thank you for this information 🙏 
Maybe you could write that the package is not ready yet on every documentation page until you have a link to the new package and docs ?

2254.title: [navigation menu] Support click-only or mixed trigger modes for item activation
2254.body:
# Feature request

## Summary

Add support for activating `NavigationMenu.Item` via **click only**, not just hover or hover+click. Ideally, allow configuring individual triggers to respond to either hover, click, or both. This would provide more control and flexibility in building complex navigation patterns.

For example, some menu items might require explicit user intent (click-only), while others benefit from faster access via hover or combined interaction.

## Examples in other libraries

Radix UI's related issue: [radix-ui/primitives#1630](https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives/issues/1630)

## Motivation

I'm building a menu with mixed interaction models — some items should open on **click only**, while others can open on **hover or click**. Currently, `NavigationMenu` doesn't offer fine-grained control over interaction type per trigger.

Supporting this would enable more accessible, predictable navigation behaviors for complex UIs, such as:

- Large dropdowns that shouldn't open on accidental hover
- Menus with nested navigation requiring deliberate activation

nodes.body: I think `click-only` is technically possible at the moment by setting `delay`/`closeDelay` to really high values?

Right now, I went through the same route as I did for Radix (removing props for click). Funny enough, removing a 'ref' gives the best result.

And yes, setting delays would help with accidental hovering but not without drawbacks: when you click, you still feel that delay.

Another option would be controlling a nav menu manually and setting on click handlers.

> And yes, setting delays would help with accidental hovering but not without drawbacks: when you click, you still feel that delay.

I'm not sure how that's possible because the click handlers don't have any `delay` - when you click, it just opens instantly. Are you referring to the click occuring on `mouseup` not `mousedown`?

> Funny enough, removing a 'ref' gives the best result.

The hover listeners are assigned via the ref, not React props, which could explain this. But still - setting the delays to really high values does solve the issue since the hover open will just be delayed "forever" and cause it to never open on hover

In my testing, `delay` only applies to hovering and not to clicking. Still, I would like this feature too.

The Menu also has `closeOnClick` which is missing from Navigation Menu (but important if your nav menu is, for example, in your Next.js layout where its state persists when clicking a link).

Sorry for the confusion. When I said “you still feel that delay,” I was referring to my specific use case — I have a few triggers that should respond to click events, in addition to the existing hover-based triggers.

Essentially, I’d like both click and hover triggers to be active at the same time, but the delay is applied globally across the entire menu, which causes issues.

> Essentially, I’d like both click and hover triggers to be active at the same time

Ah right, your description made that clear in the original post already:

> some items should open on click only, while others can open on hover or click. Currently, NavigationMenu doesn't offer fine-grained control over interaction type per trigger

I guess `NavigationMenu.Item` could have a prop such as `openOnHover={false}`. Though, I don't think it should support hover-only, because keyboard users need a way to access the content. Are just `click-only` and `hover+click` enough to satisfy your use case?

> > Essentially, I’d like both click and hover triggers to be active at the same time
> 
> Ah right, your description made that clear in the original post already:
> 
> > some items should open on click only, while others can open on hover or click. Currently, NavigationMenu doesn't offer fine-grained control over interaction type per trigger
> 
> I guess `NavigationMenu.Item` could have a prop such as `openOnHover={false}`. Though, I don't think it should support hover-only, because keyboard users need a way to access the content. Are just `click-only` and `hover+click` enough to satisfy your use case?

Yes, absolutely!
It this planned to be added? Would be great to have this feature. Many nav setups on sites are click to open only. I have a need to do this as well.

2780.title: [toast] `Portal` doesn't respect provided `container`
2780.body:
# Bug report

## Current behavior

We're providing a `container` to `Toast.Portal` but it's still using the default portal.

## Expected behavior

`Toast.Portal` honours `container` prop.

## Reproducible example

https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/kind-shadow-y4prt7?workspaceId=ws_4qKVDKGuRrrvd8oLfoRWSz

## Base UI version

For example v1.0.0-beta.3

## Which browser are you using?

Chrome

## Which OS are you using?

Mac OS

## Which assistive tech are you using (if applicable)?

N/A

## Additional context

N/A

nodes.body:
What's the reason for this:

```jsx
{...(portal ? { container: portal } : {})
```

Doing this will work:

```jsx
container={portal}
```

Internally, it checks for `null` to determine if it needs to wait for the element to exist (if it doesn't yet). `undefined` just falls back to the default behavior and doesn't check again.

It's not clear in the example I posted but we want to use the default portal element if a custom one doesn't exist yet, so that's why we can't do `container={portal}`.

FWIW I haven't noticed this problem with the portal for other components but maybe there's another reason for that.

For context, our use case for this is to support toasts above top layer dialog elements. When the dialog isn't open we can use the default portal, but when the dialog opens we set a portal element in React context (a portal inside the dialog), and we need the toasts to switch to using that (so it appears above).

I did actually try to create a reduced test case to demonstrate this but for some reason I couldn't reproduce the issue, hence why I shared a simpler reduced test case, which is hopefully sufficient to illustrate the issue.

`Tooltip`, `PreviewCard`, and `Toast` use a different "lite" component portal version, but they ultimately still uses the same portal hook as the "full" component portal (`Popover`, `Dialog`, `Menu`, `Select` etc.)

In the CodeSandbox, `container={portal}` works, so I'm not sure what you're trying to do exactly. Pass `container: null` if the element doesn't exist yet, and then pass the real element once it's been created, and the portal element will work correctly. Here's a container with a 2s timeout delay before the element gets passed: https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/tender-parm-rdsx8w

In the CodeSandbox you shared, the toast doesn't appear if you click the button before the timeout. This is the problem with passing `container={portal}` where `portal` is `null`. We want to conditionally use a custom portal. If there isn't one yet, we want to use the default portal created by Base UI.

Ok, I understand what you're trying to do. You want to use the default portal in the common case (when top-layer dialogs aren't open) but then once one opens, move the portal element into the dialog layer.

In that case `key` works:
```js
<Toast.Portal container={portal ?? undefined} key={portal}>
```

That's the workaround I settled on as well. It felt wrong but I think that's mostly because I couldn't find any documentation to explain the behaviour I was seeing:

> `undefined` just falls back to the default behavior and doesn't check again.

Would it make sense to either document this or change the behaviour so it does check again?

`container={portal ?? undefined}` doesn't work because of https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/1916, so I had to do `{...(portal ? { container: portal } : {})}`.

[Here's an example](https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/dazzling-swirles-nvzlyh?workspaceId=ws_4qKVDKGuRrrvd8oLfoRWSz) to demonstrate this issue does not reproduce with other portal components e.g. `Menu`. If you open the menu and inspect the DOM, you'll see the menu is portalled into the custom portal element, unlike in my original toast example.

3546.title: [select] Select closes immediately inside extension popup in Firefox
3546.body:
