https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives/issues/2754

nodes.body:
Yeah, you need to conditionally react to the reason/event to call `eventDetails.cancel()` or it will break regular behavior - might need a timeout to prevent closes from being blocked in general. I'll have to investigate why the Firefox extension popup is being weird regarding the events given it doesn't happen in FF generally...

I guess this is the bug on Firefox's side...:

> Upon switching from uncontrolled to controlled mode during debugging, we've noticed the onOpenChange callback is called twice upon a click interaction, confirming that the DropdownMenu is opened & closed in a very rapid succession.

> Yeah, you need to conditionally react to the reason/event to call `eventDetails.cancel()` or it will break regular behavior - might need a timeout to prevent closes from being blocked in general. I'll have to investigate why the Firefox extension popup is being weird regarding the events given it doesn't happen in FF generally...
> 
> I guess this is the bug on Firefox's side...:
> 
> > Upon switching from uncontrolled to controlled mode during debugging, we've noticed the onOpenChange callback is called twice upon a click interaction, confirming that the DropdownMenu is opened & closed in a very rapid succession.
> 
> 

I saw some other work around did use a timer to prevent closing. But not sure if this is the best way

By "conditionally", do you mean to detect if it's in Firefox extension and if yes, call eventDetails.cancel()?
By conditionally, I mean check a close event happened too soon after the open event (within 400ms), and only then cancel it

> It's emitting `window-resize` for some reason, which closes it. Preventing that works. > > > > ```jsx > items={fonts} > onOpenChange={(open, { reason, event, cancel }) => { > if (reason === 'window-resize') { > cancel() > } > setDetails({ > lastReason: reason, > lastEvent: event.type, > }) > }} > > > ``` > > What was the problem for Popover? This event doesn't happen for Popover. Does it happen for Menu too? 😅 I'm migrating from radix to base ui for https://github.com/mengxi-ream/read-frog I will provide a detailed report for these components after I done the migration

Turns out the fix in #3918 isn't enough, because for some random reason the Firefox extension popup also fires `"resize"` events when hovering over items, probably because `.focus()` is being called on them.

I'm closing this as "wontfix", as there's not a straightforward way to handle this without breaking the common normal webpage scenario in FF.

Two concrete workarounds:
1. Either call `eventDetails.cancel()` in the `onOpenChange` prop on `<Select.Root>` if `eventDetails.reason === 'window-resize'` while inside the extension popup (since resizes don't appear to be relevant in the extension popup)
2. Or, always use `alignItemWithTrigger={false}` on `<Select.Positioner>` in Firefox extension popups

---

I wasn't able to reproduce any issues with Popover inside the extension popup

1746.title: [menu] Should link menu items navigate to their `href` when pressing Space?
1746.body:
# Feature request

## Summary

[As documented](https://base-ui.com/react/components/menu#navigate-to-another-page), `Menu.Item`s can render a `<a href>` to navigate to a URL when clicked, instead of executing an action on the page. Such a menu item is highlightable in exactly the same ways that a regular menu item is, but it is not _activatable_  in the same ways: pressing the Space key doesn't navigate to the URL. It does, however, close the menu (assuming `closeOnClick` is not changed from its default value of `true`), which seems wrong. I feel like if nothing happens, it should not close the menu, but ideally, IMO it _should_ navigate to the URL, just like when pressing Enter.

[CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/naughty-mclean-p9zwrl)

## Examples in other libraries

Not sure.

## Motivation

Consistency in keyboard behavior for end users who may not understand the difference between menu items that are links and those that are not.

nodes.body:
Here is a relevant thread https://github.com/w3c/aria/issues/1701

Making `<a>` activate with Space is problematic, because Space scrolls the viewport. Users might expect Space to scroll the viewport, even when an `<a>` is focused. However, we could say this behaviour should be different inside widgets, since users generally don't expect Space to scroll the viewport when focus is inside a widget.

The main blocker is that `:active` doesn't fire when pressing Space. So if we do this, we'll need to commit to handling custom pseudo classes via data attrs. We haven't made a decision there yet.

Thanks @colmtuite. From the thread you linked to:

> If you design a link like a button (which is not a good idea) you can already make the link activatable with SPACE. **This would not be a violation of any specification** - but also not required according to WCAG 2.1.1, since WCAG does not require a specific keyboard operation, but only any.

(Emphasis mine.) In the Menu's case, a link is styled like a menu item, so I would expect it to work like other menu items. But I hear your concerns and shall await your decision!
Wow, `event.preventBaseUIHandler()` is awesome, thank you!
I find it strange that even with explicit `Menu.LinkItem`s (introduced in v1.2.0), space doesn't activate the link... seems inconsistent when every other item in a menu can be activated with space.

Given the competition: [react-aria](https://react-aria.adobe.com/Menu#links), [fluent UI](https://storybooks.fluentui.dev/react/?path=/docs/components-menu-menu--docs#menu-item-link-navigation) I would agree that activating a `MenuLinkItem` with <kbd>Space</kbd> makes sense.
We can keep the option of disabling this behavior with the `event.preventBaseUIHandler()`.
WDYT guys?
cc @mj12albert
Yep, it will be changed in https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/4053

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@4053
```

2686.title: [autocomplete] Allow submitting arbitrary value in Autocomplete with a single Enter key press
2686.body:
# Feature request

## Summary

Currently, when using the Autocomplete component and entering a custom value that is not in the suggestion list, the user must press Enter twice to submit the form: once to close the popup and a second time to actually submit. This occurs whether or not the Empty component is rendered. It would improve UX if arbitrary values could be submitted with a single Enter key press, even when the suggestions popup is open and no suggestion is selected.

## Motivation

This change would streamline the user experience for forms using Autocomplete, especially in scenarios where arbitrary values are common. It removes unnecessary interaction steps and makes the component feel more intuitive for end users.

## Sandbox

https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/mtkp2r

nodes.body:
@oltodo I thought of this while developing, and wondered if this should just be handled inside `<Autocomplete.Item onClick>` (`form.requestSubmit()`, or placing submit logic inside the click handler itself). The `onClick` fires on either mouse click or Enter selection.

This would need to be a configurable prop at least, since the autocomplete can be inside a larger form and not necessarily be the only field

@oltodo A few thoughts:

- Use `event.key` over `event.code`.
- The behavior you are looking for seems to also be the default in MUI Base / Material UI Autocomplete: https://github.com/mui/material-ui/blob/ea95d06e75c7e25c8236183310e8eda3fd07cd4a/packages/mui-material/src/useAutocomplete/useAutocomplete.js#L869. I can't find discussions about changing this behavior there, Google Search behave the proposed way (but also for values suggested), and we already have someone (you) asking about it in our new version of the component. Those are signals toward "type: bug".

> This would need to be a configurable prop at least, since the autocomplete can be inside a larger form and not necessarily be the only field

Yeah, It feels like Material UI is likely wrong in its handling. I would either expect Google Search or current Base UI. So "type: new feature" or "type: bug" depending on what best by default.

> Material UI's also prevents the submission if the popup is open. […] The user is asking to not do that

@atomiks Ah, to be clear, Material UI Autocomplete popup won't open if "entering a custom value that is not in the suggestion list. A playground https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/nifty-flower-rmvrd5.

I think the solution here is:

- By default, if there is no highlighted index, submit on Enter even if the popup is open (this is a bug)
- Provide  a prop for Autocomplete that allows always submitting on Enter even if the popup is open (highlight or not) - desirable if the autocomplete is a 1-field form

Thanks guys for answering and investigating.

> Use event.key over event.code.

Indeed

> `<Item onClick>` should be better than `onOpenChange` though to handle this.

`<Item onClick>` is only triggered when pressing Enter with a suggestion highlighted. This prevents the form from being submitted when no suggestion is highlighted or when there are no suggestions available (i.e. when `Autocomplete.Empty` is displayed).

This feature will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui-components/react@2700
```

nodes.body: > [@ozgurozalp](https://github.com/ozgurozalp) Looks like it's being blocked by Radix's DialogOverlay: > > > The [`container` prop](https://base-ui.com/react/components/combobox#ComboboxPortal-container) on ComboboxPortal can be used to portal the popup inside the dialog, here's a working sandbox: https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/friendly-mirzakhani-596rh2 This solves the problem in this way, but it feels like it might cause other issues :/

There may be some issues with CSS clipping when portaling it inside, but ultimately there's not much we can do if the interoperability problem is caused by something other than Base UI itself (where it's the other library making things a problem.)

Given Radix's Dialog is largely the same as our one, I don't see why migration to Base's Dialog is difficult in this case.

> There may be some issues with CSS clipping when portaling it inside, but ultimately there's not much we can do if the interoperability problem is caused by something other than Base UI itself (where it's the other library making things a problem.)
> 
> Given Radix's Dialog is largely the same as our one, I don't see why migration to Base's Dialog is difficult in this case.

You’re right, I could switch to Base UI’s dialog, but I don’t think everyone can make that change right away. I think it would be much better for the community if Base UI itself could come up with a solution for this.

I am facing the same issue and yes the `containerRef` workaround works.

Is there a way `Combobox.Popup` can be rendered without being in a portal? In that case, even if inside a Radix Dialog, its position would be relative to the Dialog?

I took a closer look as the Sandbox broke for me earlier, and the problem isn't that hard to workaround, but with a major caveat.

The main issue is Radix applies `pointer-events: none` to the entire body, so the combobox positioner/popup aren't interactive. If you set `pointer-events: auto` on the `Positioner`/`Popup` elements, everything is fine in terms of pointer interactivity of the items (provided each popup has the same `z-index`).

That said, I'm still not able to scroll the combobox popup maybe because of Radix's scroll lock, where it only allows things inside the dialog to be scrolled. In which case the portal container solution is the only thing that really works properly.

If the interoperability issue is caused by another library, you probably need to make an issue there. (And I know Radix is very unresponsive, that's why migrating is probably the only thing that is going to work if the scroll issue can't be solved)

> Is there a way Combobox.Popup can be rendered without being in a portal? In that case, even if inside a Radix Dialog, its position would be relative to the Dialog?

The portal is not a relevant problem here if you're using `container` and placing it inside the dialog as well. It achieves the same DOM layout (with one extra wrapper) as not having a portal at all.
It's on Radix's side, we are closing the issue.

2731.title: [combobox] No way for me to differentiate between hover and focus state on Combobox Items
2731.body:
# Feature request

Thank you for the combobox component!

I want to know how should I be styling the Combobox items to have different hover and focus styles?

As a consumer I would have thought that data-highlighted is applied on a `Combobox.Item` only when the options are made active through a keyboard.

nodes.body:
@mj12albert Thank you for your response. I actually tried that and it works but there would be a problem with this approach. We want to mimic a focus state, which obviously can't be done with a combobox item since they are never actually focused. If I do what you suggested, I will end up with my applied focus state after hovering onto the element and moving cursor away from the element. Here's a codesandbox with tailwind styles - https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/hrgdkq May I know what the thinking was behind adding the data-highlighted to the item even when the item is hovered? Could we think about also adding a `data-active` attribute? Something similar to what ariakit's [combobox](https://ariakit.org/components/combobox) does? ```
🍎 Apple
``` They apply `data-active-item` when you use keyboard or when you click the item.

We discussed this a bit and weren't sure what the use case is for differentiated `:hover` + `:focus` (virtual) styles. We followed what our `Select` component already does with one single `data-highlighted` attr for styling and no `:focus`/`:hover` styles.

Google Search uses synchronizes them together: 

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/68abfbc6-dde4-4ac1-b9ad-e1b168d7c085

But Google Chrome search doesn't:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fe32cc65-b30f-4cfb-baf7-c689e8f5a579

---

Other libraries' comboboxes also seem to do one or the other but are generally inconsistent

Thanks for sharing your thoughts! I still think it could be helpful to treat `:hover` and `:focus` differently in Combobox.

In some designs, the hover state might not have enough contrast to be easy to read, which can make it harder for some users to follow. The examples above are like Autocomplete and the value changes when the item is active, this isn't true for Combobox.

Having a clearer focus style - like the highlight Chrome uses - makes it easier for people using keyboards or anyone with low vision to tell which option is actually active and hence different states for `:hover` and `:focus` might be helpful here.

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

nodes.body:
> I suspect this might just be an expected outcome of [#3205](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/3205)

😅 yeah you're right: https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/3205#issuecomment-3534293844

The beta.4 behavior is because the label was linked to the button, but now it's linked to the hidden input for valid HTML

`<Select.Trigger>` acted this way in beta.4 as well, so it wasn't necessarily consistent across form controls before

If you use enclosing `<label>`s like on the docs, the selector is more straightforward:

```css
label:hover .radio {}
```

Enclosing `<label>`s also prevent issues with layout where pressing a gap between the label text and button control still activates the item. With siblings you need to ensure there's no gap in layout between them (e.g. padding)

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@3640
```

This doesn't seem to be fixed ([stackblitz](https://stackblitz.com/edit/vitejs-vite-joafmgbd?file=src%2FApp.tsx,package.json)). The workaround I've been using continues to work.

(I won't reopen this because the previous messages indicate this is expected)

@literalpie sorry, it's not clear from the GitHub bot message, but basically we allow native/old behavior if you intentionally render a `<button>`. 

We still prioritize supporting enclosing `<label>`s without creating invalid HTML by default (they render `<span>`s), but if you render a button (`<Radio.Root nativeButton render={<button />} />`), then you'll get the old `:hover` behavior back since the id is linked to the visible form control, at the cost of not being able to use enclosing labels

Actually, enclosing labels are supported as well without creating invalid HTML, though it requires some extra work:

```jsx
<Radio.Root 
  nativeButton 
  render={(props) => (
    <label>
      <button {...props} />
    </label>
  )}
/>
```

The hidden input now sits outside the `<label>`: https://stackblitz.com/edit/vitejs-vite-ctslar1y?file=src%2FApp.tsx,package.json

For others who may find this, I was confused why the suggested code works, but not a label wrapping the whole Radio.Root. [This JSFiddle](https://jsbin.com/fijunewiqi/edit?html,output) shows that if there's both a button and an input inside a label, the :hover pseudo class on the button won't include hover of the label. If only a button is inside the label, the pseudo class will include hovering of the label. Atomiks' suggested code would cause the hidden input to be a sibling of the label, not inside it. 

@atomiks You've been helpful so far, so sorry to ask another question. When you say

> without creating invalid HTML by default

Do you mean your suggested solution would cause invalid HTML? If not, what's the invalid html you refer to?

This is invalid HTML, because `<label>` can't contain both `<button>` and `<input>`

```jsx
<label>
  <Radio.Root nativeButton render={<button />} />
</label>
```

This is valid on the other hand, because `<label>` only contains the `<button>`:

```jsx
<Radio.Root 
  nativeButton 
  render={(props) => (
    <label>
      <button {...props} />
    </label>
  )}
/>
```

We optimized for the simpest case to work by default:

```jsx
<label>
  <Radio.Root />
</label>
```

Which is valid since it renders a `<span>` inside the `<label>` along with `<input>`. And retaining the `:hover` behavior is easy enough:

```css
label:hover .Radio {}
```

---

Your JS fiddle doesn't seem to contain code that describes your issue. I think it picks the first "form control", so `<button>` works if `<input>` is rendered after in the DOM

nodes.body:
> ## Expected behavior
> The `data-starting-style` will be applied during the whole duration of the panel animation.

`data-starting-style` is only meant to exist for one frame so that CSS can transition from that to the open styles, so it's really hard to observe the attribute in the DOM in devtools.

If you're having an issue, could you provide a repro? (e.g. stackblitz) @tomas-gerzicak

@tomas-gerzicak Why not just leave `overflow-hidden` unconditional like the docs demo styles do?

 `[data-starting-style]` is the style to transition **from** and is removed after 1 frame on state change so the CSS transition can play; it's not a style present during the whole open animation

> [@tomas-gerzicak](https://github.com/tomas-gerzicak) Why not just leave `overflow-hidden` unconditional like the docs demo styles do? > > `[data-starting-style]` is the style to transition **from** and is removed after 1 frame on state change so the CSS transition can play; it's not a style present during the whole open animation Hi we are using the collapsible in our docs and my other teams need to have a flexibility to display some old popovers and other interactive blocks, that can not be rendered at the bottom like the base-ui popovers, that is why we need this conditional overflow-hidden. So in that case, what would you recommend doing, can't we have another attribute, that would be present during the animation? Or can you at least change the documentation, because this implies, that the `data-starting-style` is present during the whole animation?

> because this implies, that the data-starting-style is present during the whole animation?

That wording is definitely imprecise and should be updated. 

The [Animations](https://base-ui.com/react/handbook/animation#css-transitions) page is accurate:

> - [data-starting-style] corresponds to the initial style to transition from.
> - [data-ending-style] corresponds to the final style to transition to.

> can't we have another attribute, that would be present during the animation

This use case seems niche to me; as you said, it's to support legacy popovers and isn't necessary when using Base UI ones. So I think manually working around it to support your use case is okay. 

`onOpenChangeComplete` would likely make it easier, though: https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/4434

> > because this implies, that the data-starting-style is present during the whole animation?
> 
> That wording is definitely imprecise and should be updated.

>

> > * [data-ending-style] corresponds to the final style to transition to.
> 
> > can't we have another attribute, that would be present during the animation
> 
> This use case seems niche to me; as you said, it's to support legacy popovers and isn't necessary when using Base UI ones. So I think manually working around it to support your use case is okay.
> 
> `onOpenChangeComplete` would likely make it easier, though: [#4434](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/4434)

Okay, thanks, but this `onOpenChangeComplete` will be available in the 1.6.0 right? 🤔

For what it's worth, the use-case @tomas-gerzicak is talking about matches my use-case that prompted #4381 almost exactly:

* `onOpenChangeComplete` is an acceptable solution, but something like `[data-animating]` would be even better

2186.title: [dialog] portal stays "mounted" when popup is removed
2186.body:
# Bug report

Its a bit hard to explain, but I've created an example here: https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/pedantic-grass-22zl8v?workspaceId=ws_9E7g8meGhxx9KyReSrgWiy

Steps to reproduce:
- Create a dialog where the `Dialog.Popup` is dynamically rendered depending on the `open`  state
- When the dialog is dismissed and the `Dialog.Popup` is switched out with anything else (in the examle its `null`)
- After the `Dialog.Popup` disappears the `Dialog.Portal` stays around indefinitely

## Current behavior

The `Dialog.Portal` stays around even when the `Dialog.Popup` is gone and thus no transition / animation can occur to unmount the `Dialog.Popup`.

## Expected behavior

> Why not conditionally render the whole portal element tree instead?

To give a bit more context, a dialog in the codebase I'm working in is implemented like this:

```ts
<Dialog content={<DialogPopupImplementation />} />
```

The `DialogPopupImplementation` component internally uses the `Dialog.Popup` element and the `Dialog` component provides the needed `Dialog.Portal` element for it. 1893.title: [dialog] Lock scroll on iOS if navbar is collapsed
1893.body:
In #1890 the scroll lock for iOS was changed to a basic technique (`overflow: hidden` on `<html>`) that only works if the navbar is expanded.

Numerous bugs and issues arise on iOS when trying to robustly lock the scroll. A solution must avoid all of these bugs to be implemented:

- Textboxes must slide into view when focused, nor cause a glitchy scroll animation
- The navbar must not force itself into view and cause layout shift
- Scroll containers must not flicker upon closing a popup when it has an exit animation

nodes.body:

Still, if other hover styles are necessary, you can increase the `z-index` of `Backdrop` and `Popup` to `1`. And ensure keepMounted elements like `Toast.Viewport` are `2`

isn't the point of base UI that it is a library of unstyled components that users can customize?

why would defining the opacity of the backdrop be a more valid customization than setting what the cursor looks like when hovering it?

what you are suggesting sounds a bit like stepping into z index hell - especially since other components are affected by the fix, such as the toast component you mention

@atomiks personally I think in terms of affordance in UI it makes sense to be consistent. If some clickable things have cursor pointer on hover, why not all? 

one could argue some things are obviously clickable and don't need the extra hint, and that any affordance benefits are irrelevant for mobile users who don't have pointers at all

but I believe in consistency so would either go with all clickable items having cursor pointer or none, and prefer to make it all

regarding backdrops specifically, there is no visual distinction between a dismissible and a non-dismissible backdrop, so this information is conveyed by the cursor when hovering it on a desktop device.

> A better workaround is removing the pointer-events from the internal backdrop when rendering your own

I'm not aware of how this is implemented internally, but it would be a lot simpler if `Dialog.Backdrop` didn't turn into two elements, with the unstylable one displaying on top of the stylable.

Question for you @marcospgp -- do you apply pointer cursor to the entire rest of the document when a select menu is open, where clicking outside of the select menu closes it? What about clicking in an empty area to un-focus some focused input element, e.g. when trying to trigger some onBlur callback when clicking outside of a "live" text input that saves your changes on blur? 

I get wanting to be consistent, but seeing a pointer on a dialog backdrop would be confusing to me, and inconsistent with every dialog interaction I can remember using. So, knock yourself out once this is supported if it's scratching some itch, but I would call this an anti-pattern that most users are not expecting.

> do you apply pointer cursor to the entire rest of the document when a select menu is open, where clicking outside of the select menu closes it? What about clicking in an empty area to un-focus some focused input element, e.g. when trying to trigger some onBlur callback when clicking outside of a "live" text input that saves your changes on blur?

@eckmLJE the scenarios you describe have one thing in common, which is that when the pointer is hovering outside the element there is not one single possible outcome. the rest of the document is still interactive, so you can't know whether the user intent would be to unfocus the current element or focus on a new one. so you can consider the unfocusing a side effect, and continue conveying information about the interactivity of other elements.

if you hover a backdrop, you can signal that it is clickable without losing any information, because clicking it only has one possible outcome.

the cursor being a pointer conveys the same information as the backdrop getting dimmer (or whatever other style one applies to it). the same reasoning applies for why not to display a backdrop when focusing on an input box, or opening a dropdown

it's just about trying to convey as much information as possible to the user while trying to balance conflicting feedback by guessing their intent

here's an example: what differentiates a backdrop that can be dismissed with a click from one that cannot?

@marcospgp I was responding to your universal consistency position:
>If some clickable things have cursor pointer on hover, why not all?

You also seem now to be explaining why not all! 😄 

Following simple common patterns for interactions like this seems obvious to me. It's a learned behavior not to click on a backdrop if I don't intend to attempt a close, whether there is a pointer cursor there or not. But I'm fine agreeing to disagree here, best wishes to your project 👍🏻

4039.title: `useRender` can crash during React reconciliation
4039.body:
# Bug report

## Current behavior

If component `CountButton` contains a React hook, then React will crash when `isCounter` changes:

```jsx
{isCounter ? (
  <Button render={CountButton} />
) : (
  <Button>No counter</Button>
)}
```

You can work around this by rendering the element before passing it in:

```jsx
{isCounter ? (
  <Button render={<CountButton />} />
) : (
  <Button>No counter</Button>
)}
```

## Expected behavior

Neither example should crash React.

## Reproducible example

https://stackblitz.com/edit/vitejs-vite-ykx9a7mz?file=src%2FApp.jsx

## Base UI version

v1.1.0

## Additional context

This is a pretty big footgun. It's apparently due to React optimistically merging the state of the same component at the same position in the tree.

These are the two errors we've seen in this scenario:

```
Minified React error #300

Rendered fewer hooks than expected. This may be caused by an accidental early return statement.
```

```
Minified React error #310

Rendered more hooks than during the previous render.
```

nodes.body: BAS-375

`render`'s function indeed doesn't support a component as a function (`render={Component}`), only these patterns are correct:

- `render={<Component />}`
- `render={(props) => <Component {...props} />}`

It can't contain hooks, etc.
Not if you break it out into its own component. AFAIK the reproduction link follows the rules of hooks, it just doesn't follow the rules of Base UI.

It might be possible to warn in dev mode if `renderFn.name` starts with an uppercase letter, though I feel like there will be potential bugs with dev-mode minification for some users' setups. Though maybe things like Fast Refresh already rely on this kind of detection

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

2293.title: [docs] Please remove "Set up portals" step
2293.body: The `root` selector that you must add to your root `layout.tsx` and `globals.css` is very random and should be completely removed and another more non-intrusive way of installing the plugin should be put in place instead. Preferably this step should be removed completely. <--🤢🤮 Also notice that I already have `:root` further down in my file. Wait, what?! Two roots?! 🤔😵‍💫🤯🤢🤮 <--🤢🤮 PS. Yes, this is actually how my brain interprets this step. I'm accustomed to having my files clean, elegant, neat and tidy, etc - don't want them polluted with nonsensical stuff. Maybe also add a "discussion" label to the issue.

nodes.body:
> The root selector that you must add to your root layout.tsx and globals.css is very random

Are you disputing the randomness of the name `.root` or the necessity of this whole step?

The doc here (that you screen capped as well) explain why this step is needed @spacecat

It's a necessity for the `isolation: isolate` style to be used somewhere within an app, so I'd advise against removing the step entirely. The docs' wording is fairly clear, too: it specifically mentions "application layout root". I'd assume it doesn't explicitly mention the usage of `:root`, `html` or `body` because if you were to apply the style to those selectors instead, you would find it difficult to integrate with a third-party library that makes use of z-index, such as react-toastify (see [this issue](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/1935)). If you're not integrating with any third party libraries this way, you could probably change it from `.root` to `:root`. 

It also doesn't need to be called `root`. It can be called whatever you want, similar to [how React apps are initialized](https://react.dev/learn/add-react-to-an-existing-project#step-2-render-react-components-anywhere-on-the-page):

```
const root = createRoot(document.getElementById('app'));
```

> Also notice that I already have :root further down in my file. Wait, what?! Two roots?! 🤔😵‍💫🤯🤢🤮 React apps do have two roots (which is more obvious in an SPA Vite app): the `` element `:root` and the React app render root (which can be called `.app` or something else.) > I'm accustomed to having my files clean, elegant, neat and tidy, etc - don't want them polluted with nonsensical stuff. I don't see how three lines "pollutes" your app. You can add a comment explaining why it's needed, though. > I'd assume it doesn't explicitly mention the usage of :root, html or body Placing the style on `body` or `html` doesn't have any effect, because Base UI's popups will just be inside the same stacking context as your app's content. There must be a separate, new stacking context created as a sibling of where Base UI popup portals get rendered so the popups are outside the app's stacking context: ```html
Always underneath
Base UI popup renders here and is always above that element
``` This enables the Base UI popup to always appear above anything inside that `isolation` container, regardless of `z-index` styles — meaning there is no need to define a `z-index` on the popups to ensure that e.g. they appear above fixed navbars or other positioned elements inside the container. Multiple Base UI popups outside of that container also naturally overlap each other correctly based on their rendering/insertion order (with `keepMounted=false`). It's rare (except when dealing with `keepMounted` elements that are opened on top of an existing popup later) that you need to touch `z-index` styles on the popups when applying this step. It's also necessary for the `modal` prop to work correctly for pointers
Since the issue is missing key information and has been inactive for 7 days, it has been automatically closed. If you wish to see the issue reopened, please provide the missing information.

Sorry for late reply.

All I was trying to say was that that entire step is bad DX!

Those types of things should be handled internally by the library.

It can't really be handled automatically in a way that always works, namely because of the unknown structure of your HTML. A couple reasons:

- We don't know what the true root of your app is. It may not be `document.body.firstElementChild` as there could be multiple siblings at that level.
- You may want to opt-out of `isolation` in some cases, or specify it on an element deeper in the tree so that fixed elements within your app take precedence or sit on the same layer as popups in some cases. Letting you control where it's placed gives you freedom at the cost of a small initial setup step.

I don't know the inner workings or nor should I need to care (as a developer). As developers all I want is a smooth install/setup experience/DX.

What you are describing to me sounds more like the exception rather than the norm. In that case the default should be no root stuff and if you for some reason need it then you could opt in.

My other argument is that the majority of libraries that I've used similar to this one don't have any extraneous and in my opinion unnecessary step.

Strangely I seem to be the only one around here who thinks this is bad DX 🤔🤣

@Cezero If you don't set `z-index` at all it renders correctly: https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/infallible-mclaren-5sygfz?file=%2Fsrc%2FApp.tsx

Is there any specific reason you need to explicitly specify `z-index`?

If you specify `z-index` on popups, those that are anchor-positioned like Select need to have the style on the `Positioner` part, not `Popup`. The `Positioner` element is what has the `absolute`/`fixed` style and is the layering container for the popup (so `<Select.Positioner className="z-60">` works.)

That said, you don't need to specify `z-index` on Dialog when following [Set up portals](https://base-ui.com/react/overview/quick-start#set-up-portals), except in some situations when specifying the `keepMounted` prop on it. In our docs, we specified `z-index: 1` on `Select.Positioner` (if the select is inside the dialog, this doesn't matter though) and `Toast.Viewport` (this one is usually global and must have the style) because those two have implicit behavior to be kept mounted in the DOM.
got it. Thank you!

3552.title: [number field] component with shadcn UI forms

nodes.body:
@rahulvils Can you provide a stackblitz or CSB reproducing this?

Possibly your `<form>` element is missing `noValidate`

`<form noValidate>` (as @mj12albert mentioned) will block native validation if it's not necessary here

If validation is warranted, the issue shown in the popup is that there's a `stepMismatch` error. The `step` property defaults to `1` so if the input value doesn't match it will trigger a validation error. `format={{ maximumFractionDigits: 0 }}` makes the input value match the default step. Since you've specified `step={0.01}`, you want fractional values, so `format={{ maximumFractionDigits: 2 }}` will make the input display `0.43` instead of `0.433333....` and pass the validation successfully.

Also, if you do want native validation, add `contain: layout` CSS around on the `<NumberField.Root>` component so the native popup roughly points to the correct area since it's trying to point to the fixed hidden input in the top-left
The config ```format={{ maximumFractionDigits: 2 }}``` and the ```noValidate``` fixed the issue.
@mj12albert @atomiks  How can I have a **NumberField** to accept only whole numbers instead of decimal values. The input element should not even accept the decimal-character(.) in the input field.
`format={{ maximumFractionDigits: 0 }}` achieves that (with `step={1}` / default). They can still type `.`/`,` etc. but it will be removed on blur.

3668.title: [docs] Add CSS-in-JS (e.g. Linaria) examples to docs

nodes.body:
Thanks for the interest, though I'm afraid this doesn't seem worth the overhead given that:
- the user base is very small compared to tailwind https://npm-stat.com/charts.html?package=linaria&package=tailwindcss&from=2024-01-01&to=2026-01-01
- it doesn't seem very actively maintained: https://github.com/callstack/linaria/issues/1437 & https://github.com/callstack/linaria/graphs/code-frequency

Just to clarify further, the reason this is controversial is that it's not just the upfront work. It then needs to be maintained going forward. And future components also need to be converted. And there's the extra cognitive load for users using the docs (which is tiny, but still).

Linaria has such a tiny user base, that the ROI is not there. In order to be worthwhile, a styling solution needs to a) have massive adoption and b) have a growing user base.

The npm-stat link sent above is misleading. It doesn't accurately reflect the actual usage, which is actually much larger than what is shown on the initial link. See this [comparison of `@linaria/core` vs `linaria`](https://npm-stat.com/charts.html?package=%40linaria%2Fcore&package=linaria&from=2024-01-01&to=2026-01-01) (400k downloads/week vs 7k/week). I can confidently call 400k downloads/week a massive adoption, and it's definitely growing.

The thing with Linaria is that it's just a quite thin wrapper around `@wyw-in-js` family, which is very well maintained, and so Linaria is more or less feature complete and doesn't need constant improvements. `Linaria` is `styled-components` with lessons learned. It was built with all the knowledge that was accumulated while building `styled-components`. The knowledge not to rely on `React.useContext` everywhere, for example

There are basically 2 reasons Linaria has much smaller adoption compared to something like Tailwind:
1. Tailwind's centralization - the approach is the package. There are not many alternatives (probably UnoCSS is the only one) that involve writing a ton of small classes directly inside the class attributes of JSX elements. On the other hand, the whole CSS-in-JS ecosystem has many packages that look almost the same in terms of UX, and often indistinguishable from each other. However, their stats will still be counted independently. Often, you would be able to just replace the imports without any other changes in the code `import { styled } from '@linaria/react'`, `import styled from '@emotion/styled'`, `import styled from 'styled-components'`, `import { styled } from "next-yak";`, `import { styled } from '@compiled/react';`. On the other hand, you cannot do the same with Tailwind, because the whole approach IS the package.
2. `Styled-components` is the Tailwind of CSS-in-JS ecosystem, meaning it was the thing that suddenly exploded in popularity and was a thing everybody talked about. Linaria is the successor to `styled-components`. `styled-components` was deprecated very recently ([blog post with a ton of relevant context](https://opencollective.com/styled-components/updates/thank-you)), so all of the users are still there, and have not yet moved to better-maintained packages. When you look at the [comparison of `styled-components` and `tailwindcss`](https://npm-stat.com/charts.html?package=styled-components&package=tailwindcss&from=2024-01-01&to=2026-01-01), it suddenly doesn't seem that small, when Tailwind has 4 times the traffic.

> And there's the extra cognitive load for users using the docs (which is tiny, but still). This way, absolutely any PR that adds anything to the docs increases the cognitive load on users. All of the load is another option of the `select`, which would never be the default, and may not even be acknowledged by Tailwind/CSS-modules users that it exists. I don't think it's a big deal at all to people who don't write in a CSS-in-JS approach. However, it's a big deal for people like me who actually build using the CSS-in-JS approach daily So, my asking to add Linaria is basically me asking to add a CSS-in-JS option, but adding `styled-components` even if widely adopted doesn't make sense, because `styled-components` doesn't work well in the latest React ecosystem, like server components, so I suggested the next best option. The trend of Linaria is going upwards

On the future maintainability side, there are 2 potential solutions.
1. We can find a future code-owner (I can do this as well) in the community on Discord or on X
2. I can write an auto-generator from CSS Modules, and it should not take more than 500 lines of code to generate CSS-in-JS examples continuously. Even better, it could obsolete the need to maintain CSS module examples because they could be generated from Tailwind examples as well.

I've just realized a few factors that could help move this forward.

Under the MUI organization, there's a CSS-in-JS library called Pigment CSS, and in its sources, I found a few interesting details:

[HOW_PIGMENT_CSS_WORKS.md](https://github.com/mui/pigment-css/blob/7d763a05bac5fef24f0566bbbd52f7e003b8230c/HOW_PIGMENT_CSS_WORKS.md):
> Pigment CSS uses the [WyW-in-JS](https://wyw-in-js.dev/) library that also powers [Linaria](https://linaria.dev/).

[pigment-css-vite-plugin/src/vite-plugin.ts](https://github.com/mui/pigment-css/blob/7d763a05bac5fef24f0566bbbd52f7e003b8230c/packages/pigment-css-vite-plugin/src/vite-plugin.ts):
> Extended from https://github.com/callstack/linaria/blob/master/packages/vite/src/index.ts for it be customizable in future

[pigment-css-react/src/processors/base-processor.ts](https://github.com/mui/pigment-css/blob/7d763a05bac5fef24f0566bbbd52f7e003b8230c/packages/pigment-css-react/src/processors/base-processor.ts#L25) twice:
>  // Implementation taken from Linaria https://github.com/callstack/linaria/blob/master/packages/react/src/processors/styled.ts#L284

If Linaria is still not something you're willing to consider, examine the possibility of adding examples with in-house Pigment CSS, or with [`next-yak`](https://github.com/DigitecGalaxus/next-yak/) ([comparison of next-yak with the whole CSS-in-JS ecosystem](https://yak.js.org/docs/comparison)), which is closer to the syntax I'm advocating for and grows quite quickly recently

Also, I'm looking forward to your feedback on this
> I can write an auto-generator from CSS Modules, and it should not take more than 500 lines of code to generate CSS-in-JS examples continuously. Even better, it could obsolete the need to maintain CSS module examples because they could be generated from Tailwind examples as well.

About the user base and maintenance:

There doesn't seem to be an alternative to Linaria-like approach, in terms of allowing zero-runtime CSS-in-JS. Even if the user base was small, it would still make sense to include.

Personally, I've paired Linaria with Capsize CSS and a DX-friendly wrapper over CSS clamp(), and I'm not sure how I would use Capsize otherwise without extra boilerplate, plugins, and maintenance. I have all the typography defined in simple JS that is then transformed with scripts, resulting in pixel-perfect responsive typography matching Figma designs. Which basically erased the need for writing weird SCSS formulas, or PostCSS plugins with custom CSS-like syntax.
This is just one game-changing thing, but generally Linaria is powering most of the responsiveness, animations, and constraints in my apps, so that the design stage is cleanly separated from the programming stage.

I don't see how I would continue trying to work around CSS, after I knew there's an option to make it completely scriptable, without the need for separate community packages, syntax, and logic.
100% type-safety, full autocomplete, and component/wrapper-based approach (as opposed to class-based approach) just sold CSS-in-JS to me, at least until Tailwind or CSS Modules mature enough to have that out of the box.

Regarding Linaria specifically, it's been some years, so it may not the best solution anymore, due to e.g. long build times (not even sure if this holds true now). Its base, ([WyW-in-JS](https://wyw-in-js.dev/)), is somewhat actively maintained, looks like it's making improvements in framework support and performance.

3725.title: <Select/> `base-select` component is unresponsive when used inside a `Drawer` component

nodes.body: Root Cause: Z-Index Conflict Testing confirmed that the z-50 style applied to the Drawer component caused context stacking issues. The SelectPositioner's default z-index was insufficiently high, resulting in the dropdown list triggering but being obscured by the Drawer layer. Recommendation: Force z-50 in the SelectPositioner's styles to ensure it displays correctly above the Drawer. Note: Hard-coding values in code is generally considered poor practice.

Hello @WuChenDi, I'm glad you were able to find the root cause of the problem.
Could you clarify if you still see any problems with the component?
It doesn't look like we set any explicit `z-index` of our own. It's up to the user to ensure the expected `z-index` value given their application context.

`z-index` isn't the only issue though. Even when it's visually on a higher layer, it's unresponsive, because Radix dialog (which is used by the Drawer in shadcn) sets `pointer-events: none` on the `<body>` to enforce "modality" but this breaks interoperability with other libraries, like us. 

The solution is to specify `container` on the portal - `<Select.Portal container={drawerContentElement}>` - to point to the Drawer content/popup

See https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives/issues/3694

@atomiks WDYT, would it be worth adding a section in the component page or a separate page (FAQ or something alike) with such common problems?
This is a second or third issue related to the same root cause that I can recall. 🙈
Since the issue is missing key information and has been inactive for 7 days, it has been automatically closed. If you wish to see the issue reopened, please provide the missing information.

2938.title: [Bug][Dialog] Not able to use sonner/react-hot-toast etc with dialog component
2938.body:
# Bug report

## Current behavior

- I am using base ui dialog in 2 of my projects
- one repo uses sonner for toasts another one uses react hot toast
- when i trigger a toast while the dialog is open toast is always behind the backdrop

## Expected behavior

- toast should render on top
- I am migrating dialog from radix to base -> it works fine in radix dialog

## Base UI version

1.0.0-beta.3

## Which browser are you using?

Chrome

## Which OS are you using?

Mac OS

nodes.body:
You need to set a `z-index` on the toasts to be higher than the dialog and ensure the toasts are portaled _outside_ your root app element. 

`position: relative; z-index: 1` will work fine (this is what our Toast demo does) provided the toast viewports of sonner and react-hot-toast are outside of your main root layout element that has `isolation: isolate` applied. Base UI has no opinions regarding stacking order, aside from [this recommendation](https://base-ui.com/react/overview/quick-start#portals) for portals. However this can cause toasts rendered _inside_ that element to always be stuck "behind" the Base UI portaled dialog, so you must make sure toasts are outside of that container as well.

That should fix it, but the example you gave is not really a reproducible example given this is interaction between multiple dependencies and the overall app layout's CSS stacking order
Yeah I figured it was inside of it, in which case `z-index` doesn't work no matter how high the value

1935.title: [toast] How to make a non base ui component overlap the dialog
1935.body:
Im using the react toastify library and when my dialog is open the toast component is beneath the backdrop. Not sure how to go about this but there are certain components that i need to be on top of all the others that may not be a part of base ui library. For some other use cases i used the dialog to overlap another dialog but i can't do the same for this one. A bit of context, this is my layout.tsx: ```
{children}
``` So i think, because of the `.Root` rule anything else is beneath the dialog but is there a way to teleport some other components to the baseui portal?

nodes.body: The `ToastContainer` needs to be outside the `isolation: isolate` `Root` container. Then assuming it has a `z-index`, it will appear above any Base UI popups, even if it's before them in the DOM.
Yeah, that was it, thank you.

4329.title: Export Root context hooks for better composability
4329.body:
# Feature request

## Summary

Expose **Root context hooks** for Base UI components to allow developers to access internal component state and behaviors when building custom subcomponents.

Currently, many Base UI components manage their state internally through a Root provider (e.g. `open`, `value`, `active item`, `disabled`, etc.), but the corresponding context hooks are not publicly exported. Providing access to these hooks would enable advanced composition while still leveraging Base UI’s built-in logic and accessibility features.

For example, hooks such as:

- `useCheckboxRootContext()`
- `useSwitchRootContext()`

would allow developers to build custom triggers, indicators, animations, or wrappers that interact with the component state.

---


## Motivation

When building real-world applications or internal design systems, developers often need to:

- Create **custom triggers or controls**
- Add **custom animations or transitions**
- Build **design system wrappers**
- Access state such as:
  - `open`
  - `selected value`
  - `active item`
  - `disabled`

Without access to the Root context, developers may need to:

- Re-implement component logic

Exposing official context hooks would improve:

- **Composability**
- **Extensibility**
- **Developer experience**
- **Design system integration**

while still keeping the internal implementation consistent and accessible.

nodes.body: BAS-467
Can you provide one example where it's needed?

> I need state in checkbox indicator to animate indicator with motion.

This is already possible, the recommended way is using [the `render` prop](https://base-ui.com/react/handbook/composition#render-function) where the `state` contains all the state that you would need:
```tsx
<Checkbox.Indicator
  keepMounted
  render={(props, state) => (
    <motion.svg
      {...props}
      animate={state.checked || state.indeterminate ? "checked" : "unchecked"}
      fill="none"
      initial={false}
      stroke="currentColor"
      strokeWidth="4"
      viewBox="0 0 24 24"
      xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
    >
      <motion.path
        d={state.indeterminate ? "M5 12h14" : "M4.5 12.75l6 6 9-13.5"}
        strokeLinecap="round"
        strokeLinejoin="round"
        variants={tickVariants}
      />
    </motion.svg>
  )}
/>
```

For `checked ? <SunIcon /> : <MoonIcon />` see this demo: https://base-ui.com/react/components/toggle

@adityaa72 Do you have a use-case where this isn't sufficient?

Thanks for the explanation. You're correct that this is possible with the render prop, but there are still use-cases where context hooks provide a better developer experience.

The render prop only exposes state within the render callback. This makes it difficult to build reusable components that consume the component state elsewhere in the subtree.

For example:

```tsx
function MenuAnalytics() {
  const { open } = useMenuRootContext()

  useEffect(() => {
    if (open) track("menu_open")
  }, [open])

  return null
}
```

Usage:
``` tsx
<Menu.Root>
  <Menu.Trigger />
  <Menu.Content />
  <MenuAnalytics />
</Menu.Root>
```

With the render prop approach, this logic must live inside a slot render function instead of an independent component.

While this could technically be implemented by using a render prop on `Menu.Root`, it would require restructuring the component tree and moving unrelated logic into the render callback, which makes reusable abstractions and separation of concerns harder.

---

Another advantage of consuming state through context is that it keeps component composition simpler.

For example, your suggestion works well when the logic stays inside the render callback. However, in many cases we want to compose custom components directly as children:

```tsx
<Checkbox.Indicator keepMounted>
  <MyCustomIcon />
</Checkbox.Indicator>
```

Where MyCustomIcon can internally read the state:
```tsx
function MyCustomIcon() {
  const { checked, indeterminate } = useCheckboxRootContext()

  return checked || indeterminate ? <CheckedIcon /> : <UncheckedIcon />
}
```

This allows the component tree to remain declarative while keeping the state-dependent logic inside reusable components.

With the render prop approach, the logic must remain inline in the render function, which makes extracting reusable components harder and couples the implementation more tightly to the slot.

We don't support this in favor of the render prop
```
<Checkbox.Indicator keepMounted>
  <MyCustomIcon />
</Checkbox.Indicator>
```

For the other cases like "MenuAnalytics", you should use controlled mode

We may consider providing context access in the future, but IMO it's unlikely we will do so in v1

Just adding some context for future consideration, since I noticed a similar pattern while looking at the upcoming Calendar component work and thought it was relevant to this discussion. In Calendar, the current approach is to expose `Calendar.useContext()` inside the subtree so custom parts can read state like `visibleDate `and call actions such as `setVisibleDate().` That seems related to the feature being requested here. If this kind of API is ever considered in the future for other components, a similar context-based approach could be one way to support those composition use cases.

We have an ongoing discussion about exposing this kind of hook.
On the Calendar, I decided to add one for the `visibleDate` / `setVisibleDate` because those props are very often used (to create headers mostly) and I didn't want people to have to control the prop and to use custom context or props drilling all the time.
But this is slightly inconsistent with the other Base UI components where the solutions would have been to control `visibleDate` and to pass `setVisibleDate` manually to the header.

The Calendar is not released yet and we'll try to unify the approaches before releasing it.
The last thing I want is to end up with DX that vary randomly across components without a clear reason.

If you have use cases that you think can help us understand why this is needed (what you need in this context exactly) please explain them here so that we can take the best decision possible.
